1227	[Ambient image identity]
1228	  \label{lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq}
1229	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_inf_degree_eq}
1230	  \uses{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1231	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N \subseteq M\) be homogeneous. Then the
1232	  degree-\((n+1)\) part of the image \(x \cdot N\) is exactly \(x\) of the
1233	  degree-\(n\) part of \(N\):
1234	  \[
1235	    (x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1} \;=\; x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n) .
1236	  \]
1237	\end{lemma}
1238	
1239	\begin{proof}
1240	  \uses{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1241	  \(\supseteq\): for \(m \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), \(x m \in x \cdot N\) and \(x m
1242	  \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) since \(x\) raises degree. \(\subseteq\): an element \(x m
1243	  \in x \cdot N\) lying in \(\mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) equals its own degree-\((n+1)\)
1244	  component \(\mathrm{proj}_{n+1}(x m) = x(\mathrm{proj}_n(m))\)
1245	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}); homogeneity of \(N\) puts
1246	  \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), so \(x m \in x \cdot (N \cap
1247	  \mathcal{M}_n)\). The image \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous by
1248	  \cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}.
1249	\end{proof}
1250	
1251	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1252	[Ambient distributive law]
1253	  \label{lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1254	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.sup_inf_degree_eq}
1255	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous,
1256	    lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1257	  For homogeneous submodules \(P, Q \subseteq M\) and any degree \(k\), intersecting
1258	  the sum with a graded piece distributes:
1259	  \[
1260	    (P + Q) \cap \mathcal{M}_k \;=\; (P \cap \mathcal{M}_k) + (Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k) .
1261	  \]
1262	\end{lemma}
1263	
1264	\begin{proof}
1265	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous,
1266	    lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1267	  \(\supseteq\) is the general lattice inequality. For \(\subseteq\), take \(z = p +
1268	  q \in (P + Q) \cap \mathcal{M}_k\) with \(p \in P\), \(q \in Q\). Since \(z \in
1269	  \mathcal{M}_k\), \(z = \mathrm{proj}_k(z) = \mathrm{proj}_k(p) +
1270	  \mathrm{proj}_k(q)\); homogeneity of \(P\) and \(Q\)
1271	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}) puts \(\mathrm{proj}_k(p) \in P \cap
1272	  \mathcal{M}_k\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_k(q) \in Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k\), so \(z \in (P
1273	  \cap \mathcal{M}_k) + (Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k)\). The intersection and sum stay in
1274	  the homogeneous lattice by \cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous} and
1275	  \cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}.
1276	\end{proof}
1277	
1278	\subsubsection*{Closure under kernel and cokernel}
1279	
1280	The kernel/cokernel closure of the subquotient class is realised by eight ambient
1281	component facts: for a degree-raising endomorphism \(x\) and a homogeneous pair
1282	\(N' \le N\), the kernel pair is \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and the cokernel pair is
1283	\((N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\). The lemmas below record that both new pairs are homogeneous,
1284	nest correctly, are annihilated by \(x\), and are preserved by any endomorphism \(t\)
1285	commuting with \(x\) that preserves the original pair.
1286	
1287	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1288	[Homogeneity of the kernel pair's lower module]
1289	  \label{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous}
1290	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_isHomogeneous}
1291	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}
1292	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one (\cref{def:graded_raisesDegree}) and let \(N, N'\) be
1293	  homogeneous submodules of \(M\). Then the kernel pair's lower module
1294	  \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous. Indeed it is the intersection
1295	  (\cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}) of the homogeneous \(N\) with the homogeneous
1296	  preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}).
1297	\end{lemma}
1298	
1299	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1300	[Homogeneity of the cokernel pair's upper module]
1301	  \label{lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous}
1302	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_isHomogeneous}
1303	  \uses{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1304	  Under the same hypotheses, the cokernel pair's upper module \(N' + x \cdot N\) is
1305	  homogeneous: it is the sum (\cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}) of the homogeneous
1306	  \(N'\) with the homogeneous image \(x \cdot N\)
1307	  (\cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}).
1308	\end{lemma}
1309	
1310	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1311	[The kernel pair nests]
1312	  \label{lem:graded_ker_le}
1313	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_le}
1314	  If \(N' \le N\) and \(x\) preserves \(N'\) (\(x \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)), then
1315	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\). Indeed \(N' \le N\) by hypothesis, and \(x \cdot N'
1316	  \subseteq N'\) is exactly \(N' \subseteq x^{-1}(N')\); so \(N'\) is below the
1317	  intersection. Hence the kernel pair \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) is a valid
1318	  subquotient pair (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}).
1319	\end{lemma}
1320	
1321	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1322	[The cokernel pair nests]
1323	  \label{lem:graded_coker_le}
1324	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_le}
1325	  If \(N' \le N\) and \(x\) preserves \(N\) (\(x \cdot N \subseteq N\)), then
1326	  \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\): both summands lie in \(N\). Hence the cokernel pair
1327	  \((N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) is a valid subquotient pair
1328	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}).
1329	\end{lemma}
1330	
1331	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1332	[$x$ annihilates the kernel subquotient]
1333	  \label{lem:graded_ker_annihilate}
1334	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_annihilate}
1335	  The endomorphism \(x\) carries the kernel pair's lower module into \(N'\):
1336	  \(x \cdot (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \subseteq N'\). Indeed \(N \cap x^{-1}(N') \subseteq
1337	  x^{-1}(N')\), and applying \(x\) to a preimage of \(N'\) lands in \(N'\). Thus \(x\)
1338	  acts as zero on the kernel subquotient.
1339	\end{lemma}
1340	
1341	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1342	[$x$ annihilates the cokernel subquotient]
1343	  \label{lem:graded_coker_annihilate}
1344	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_annihilate}
1345	  The endomorphism \(x\) carries \(N\) into the cokernel pair's upper module:
1346	  \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\), trivially (the right summand). Thus \(x\)
1347	  acts as zero on the cokernel subquotient \(N/(N' + x \cdot N)\).
1348	\end{lemma}
1349	
1350	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1351	[Commuting endomorphisms preserve the preimage]
1352	  \label{lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute}
1353	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.comap_map_le_of_commute}
1354	  Let \(t\) commute with \(x\) and preserve \(N'\) (\(t \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)). Then
1355	  \(t\) preserves the preimage: \(t \cdot x^{-1}(N') \subseteq x^{-1}(N')\). Indeed for
1356	  \(m\) with \(x m \in N'\) one has \(x(t m) = t(x m) \in t \cdot N' \subseteq N'\), so
1357	  \(t m \in x^{-1}(N')\). This is what shows the remaining \(t_i\) preserve the kernel
1358	  pair's lower module \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\).
1359	\end{lemma}
1360	
1361	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1362	[Commuting endomorphisms preserve the image]
1363	  \label{lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute}
1364	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_map_le_of_commute}
1365	  Let \(t\) commute with \(x\) and preserve \(N\) (\(t \cdot N \subseteq N\)). Then
1366	  \(t\) preserves the image: \(t \cdot (x \cdot N) \subseteq x \cdot N\). Indeed for
1367	  \(m \in N\) one has \(t(x m) = x(t m)\) with \(t m \in N\), so \(t(x m) \in x \cdot
1368	  N\). This is what shows the remaining \(t_i\) preserve the cokernel pair's upper
1369	  module \(N' + x \cdot N\).
1370	\end{lemma}
1371	
1372	\begin{lemma}[Kernel/cokernel closure of the subquotient class]
1373	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}
1374	  % This is a narrative grouping lemma: its content is realised in Lean by the eight
1375	  % ambient component facts cited in \uses{} below (no single Lean declaration),
1376	  % together with the SubquotientDatum.ker/.coker constructors that bundle them into a
1377	  % length-(r-1) datum.
1378	  \uses{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous,
1379	    lem:graded_ker_le, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_ker_annihilate,
1380	    lem:graded_coker_annihilate, lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute,
1381	    lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute,
1382	    def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1383	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1384	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1385	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1386	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1387	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
1388	  endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\), and set \(x = t_{r-1}\). Form the two new
1389	  pairs of homogeneous submodules of \(M\)
1390	  \[
1391	    K \;=\; \bigl(\,N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\ N'\,\bigr),
1392	    \qquad
1393	    C \;=\; \bigl(\,N,\ N' + x \cdot N\,\bigr),
1394	  \]
1395	  where \(x^{-1}(N') = \{\,m \in M : x \cdot m \in N'\,\}\) is the preimage and
1396	  \(x \cdot N\) is the image of \(N\) under \(x\). Then:
1397	  \begin{enumerate}
1398	    \item \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N\) are homogeneous submodules of
1399	      \(M\), with \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\), so both
1400	      \(K\) and \(C\) are subquotient pairs;
1401	    \item the remaining endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\) pairwise commute,
1402	      raise degree by one, and preserve both members of \(K\) and of \(C\); and
1403	    \item \(x\) annihilates both subquotients: \(x \cdot (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \subseteq N'\)
1404	      and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\).
1405	  \end{enumerate}
1406	  Consequently \(K\) and \(C\) are again subquotient data, now of length \(r-1\)
1407	  (the endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\)), and \(K = \ker x\) and \(C =
1408	  \operatorname{coker} x\) on the subquotient \(N/N'\): \(K\) represents
1409	  \(\ker\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\) and \(C\) represents
1410	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\). No quotient or subtype
1411	  carrier is constructed; \(K\) and \(C\) are ambient pairs of homogeneous
1412	  submodules of the fixed \(M\).
1413	\end{lemma}
1414	
1415	\begin{proof}
1416	  \uses{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous,
1417	    lem:graded_ker_le, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_ker_annihilate,
1418	    lem:graded_coker_annihilate, lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute,
1419	    lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute,
1420	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1421	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1422	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1423	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1424	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1425	  \emph{Homogeneity.} The preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous: if
1426	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(x \cdot m \in N'\), then since \(x\) raises
1427	  degree by one (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) the components
1428	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) lie in distinct degrees, and
1429	  homogeneity of \(N'\) (split \(G_1\),
1430	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) forces each
1431	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in N'\), i.e.\ \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in x^{-1}(N')\).
1432	  Thus \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is an intersection of homogeneous submodules, hence
1433	  homogeneous. The image \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous with degree-\((n+1)\) piece
1434	  \(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) by
1435	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}, and a sum of homogeneous
1436	  submodules is homogeneous (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib} applied at the
1437	  module level), so \(N' + x \cdot N\) is homogeneous. The inclusions
1438	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (as \(x \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)) and
1439	  \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\) (as \(N' \le N\) and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N\)) are
1440	  immediate. In the language of the ambient homogeneity calculus, this paragraph is
1441	  exactly: \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous (\cref{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}),
1442	  \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous (\cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}), and the
1443	  homogeneous lattice is closed under intersection
1444	  (\cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}) and sum
1445	  (\cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}).
1446	
1447	  \emph{Preservation.} Each \(t_i\) with \(i \le r-2\) commutes with \(x\) and
1448	  preserves \(N, N'\); hence it preserves \(x^{-1}(N')\) (if \(x t_i m = t_i x m \in
1449	  N'\) whenever \(x m \in N'\)), so it preserves \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\), and it
1450	  preserves \(x \cdot N\) (as \(t_i x N = x t_i N \subseteq x N\)), so it preserves
1451	  \(N' + x \cdot N\). Degree-raising and commutativity are inherited.
1452	
1453	  \emph{Annihilation by \(x\).} For \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) one has
1454	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\) by definition, so \(x\) carries the first member of \(K\) into
1455	  its second; and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\) trivially. Thus \(x\) acts as
1456	  zero on \(N/N'\) restricted to \(K\) and on the cokernel quotient \(C\).
1457	
1458	  \emph{Identification with \(\ker x\) and \(\operatorname{coker} x\).} On the
1459	  subquotient \(N/N'\), the class of \(m \in N\) is killed by \(x\) iff
1460	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\), i.e.\ iff \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\); so \(\ker(x : N/N' \to
1461	  N/N')\) is exactly \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'))/N'\), represented by \(K\). The image of
1462	  \(x\) on \(N/N'\) is \((N' + x \cdot N)/N'\), so the cokernel
1463	  \((N/N')/\operatorname{im} x = N/(N' + x \cdot N)\) is represented by \(C\).
1464	\end{proof}
1465	
1466	\subsubsection*{The subquotient degreewise difference identity ($D_6$)}
1467	
1468	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1469	[$D_6$ -- subquotient degreewise difference]
1470	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}
1471	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_degreewise_diff}
1472	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1473	    lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1474	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq,
1475	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq,
1476	    lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1477	  % NOTE: The proof factors the two kernel-dimension computations through a
1478	  %   helper that identifies the dimension of the preimage of a submodule under the
1479	  %   inclusion of an ambient submodule with the dimension of their ambient intersection.
1480	  %   This helper is an implementation detail and carries no public blueprint node.
1481	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
1482	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
1483	  % 00K1), proof (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13939-L13947).
1484	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (degreewise short exact sequence).}
1485	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
1486	  Hilbert function \(h_M\), and let \(x = t_{r-1}\). Let \(h_K\) and \(h_C\) be the
1487	  Hilbert functions of the kernel and cokernel subquotients
1488	  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) of
1489	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}. Then for every \(n\)
1490	  \[
1491	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
1492	  \]
1493	  Writing \(Q_n = (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) for the
1494	  degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\) (so \(\dim_\kappa Q_n = h_M(n)\)), multiplication by
1495	  \(x\) is a \(\kappa\)-linear map \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\) with
1496	  \(\ker(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\) the degree-\(n\) piece of \(K\) and
1497	  \(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im} x\) the degree-\((n+1)\) piece of \(C\); the identity is
1498	  then the degreewise rank--nullity difference \(D_5\)
1499	  (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}). Specialising to \((N, N') = (\top,
1500	  \bot)\) recovers the classical \(0 \to \mathcal{M}_n \xrightarrow{x}
1501	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1} \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x\mathcal{M}_n \to 0\).
1502	\end{lemma}
1503	
1504	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, inductive step (read
1505	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13939-L13947).
1506	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
1507	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
1508	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
1509	%  following short exact sequence
1510	%  $$
1511	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
1512	%  $$
1513	%  This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
1514	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
1515	\begin{proof}
1516	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker, lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff,
1517	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq,
1518	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq,
1519	    lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1520	  Since \(N' \le N\) are homogeneous, the split \(G_1\)
1521	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) identifies the degree-\(n\) piece
1522	  of \(N/N'\) with \(Q_n = (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\), a
1523	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space of dimension \(h_M(n)\). Because \(x\)
1524	  raises degree by one and preserves \(N\) and \(N'\)
1525	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}), it induces a \(\kappa\)-linear
1526	  map \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\).
1527	
1528	  \emph{Kernel.} A class \([m] \in Q_n\) (with \(m \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\)) lies in
1529	  \(\ker(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\) iff \(x \cdot m \in N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\), i.e.\
1530	  iff \(m \in (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \cap \mathcal{M}_n\). Hence \(\ker(x : Q_n \to
1531	  Q_{n+1}) = \bigl((N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \cap \mathcal{M}_n\bigr)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1532	  is exactly the degree-\(n\) piece of \(K\), of dimension \(h_K(n)\)
1533	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}).
1534	
1535	  \emph{Cokernel.} The image of \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\) is
1536	  \(\bigl(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n) + (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\bigr)/(N' \cap
1537	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\). Using the ambient identity \((N' + x \cdot N) \cap
1538	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1} = (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}) + x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1539	  -- which is the ambient distributive law (\cref{lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq})
1540	  applied to \(P = N'\), \(Q = x \cdot N\), combined with the ambient image identity
1541	  \((x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1} = x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1542	  (\cref{lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq}) -- the cokernel \(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im} x = (N \cap
1543	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1})/\bigl((N' + x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\bigr)\) is the
1544	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece of \(C\), of dimension \(h_C(n+1)\).
1545	
1546	  \emph{Difference.} Apply the degreewise rank--nullity difference \(D_5\)
1547	  (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) to \(\varphi = (x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\),
1548	  with \(V = Q_n\), \(W = Q_{n+1}\):
1549	  \[
1550	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n)
1551	      = \dim_\kappa Q_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa Q_n
1552	      = \dim_\kappa(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)
1553	      = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
1554	  \]
1555	  This is the \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) hypothesis consumed by \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}.
1556	\end{proof}
1557	
1558	\subsubsection*{The free polynomial-ring module structure}
1559	
1560	The finiteness condition of a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) and
1561	its transfer down one generator (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) are
1562	phrased over the \emph{free} polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] =
1563	\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0, \dots, r-1\}, \kappa)\). The following blocks build the
1564	action of that ring on the ambient module \(M\) from the \(r\) pairwise-commuting
1565	degree-raising endomorphisms \(t_i\), keeping every carrier an ambient submodule of
1566	\(M\). The free polynomial ring -- rather than the subalgebra
1567	\(\mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\} \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\)
1568	-- is used precisely so the inductive transfer has the ring surjection
1569	\(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\) available
1570	(\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}).
1571	
1572	\begin{definition}\leanok
1573	[Polynomial evaluation ring homomorphism]
1574	  \label{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1575	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom}
1576	  \uses{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}
1577	  Let \(t : \{0, \dots, r-1\} \to \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) be a family of
1578	  pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms. The \emph{polynomial evaluation
1579	  ring homomorphism}
1580	  \[
1581	    \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t) : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \longrightarrow
1582	      \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)
1583	  \]
1584	  sends each variable \(X_i\) to \(t_i\). It is constructed by evaluating into the
1585	  \emph{commutative} \(\kappa\)-subalgebra \(A = \mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa(\{t_i\})
1586	  \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) -- commutative by
1587	  \cref{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib} because the generators commute -- and then
1588	  including \(A \hookrightarrow \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\); evaluation into the
1589	  noncommutative \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) directly is not an algebra
1590	  homomorphism, which is why the commutative \(A\) is interposed.
1591	\end{definition}
1592	
1593	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1594	[Evaluation sends $X_i$ to $t_i$]
1595	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}
1596	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_X}
1597	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1598	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(X_i) = t_i\) for each \(i\). Immediate from the construction
1599	  (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}), as evaluation sends \(X_i\) to the chosen image
1600	  \(t_i\).
1601	\end{lemma}
1602	
1603	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1604	[Evaluation sends a constant to a scalar]
1605	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1606	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_C}
1607	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1608	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(C\,c) = c \cdot \mathrm{id}_M\) for each \(c \in \kappa\):
1609	  the evaluation homomorphism (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}) is \(\kappa\)-linear and
1610	  carries the constant polynomial \(C\,c\) to the scalar endomorphism \(c \cdot 1\).
1611	\end{lemma}
1612	
1613	\begin{definition}\leanok
1614	[Polynomial-ring module structure on $M$]
1615	  \label{def:graded_polyModule}
1616	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule}
1617	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1618	  The \emph{polynomial-module structure} is the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-module
1619	  structure on \(M\) obtained by restricting scalars along
1620	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)\) (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}): a polynomial \(p\) acts on
1621	  \(m \in M\) by \(p \cdot m = \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(p)(m)\). This is the module over the
1622	  free polynomial ring on which the ambient subquotient induction's finiteness condition
1623	  is measured.
1624	\end{definition}
1625	
1626	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1627	[$X_i$ acts as $t_i$]
1628	  \label{lem:graded_polyModule_X_smul}
1629	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule_X_smul}
1630	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}
1631	  In the polynomial-module structure (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}), \(X_i \cdot m =
1632	  t_i(m)\) for all \(m \in M\). Immediate from
1633	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(X_i) = t_i\) (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}).
1634	\end{lemma}
1635	
1636	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1637	[A constant acts as a scalar]
1638	  \label{lem:graded_polyModule_C_smul}
1639	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule_C_smul}
1640	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1641	  In the polynomial-module structure (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}), \((C\,c) \cdot m =
1642	  c \cdot m\) for all \(c \in \kappa\), \(m \in M\). Immediate from
1643	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(C\,c) = c \cdot \mathrm{id}_M\)
1644	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}).
1645	\end{lemma}
1646	
1647	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1648	[$\kappa$-scalar tower]
1649	  \label{lem:graded_polyModule_isScalarTower}
1650	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule_isScalarTower}
1651	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1652	  The polynomial-module structure (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}) is compatible with the
1653	  original \(\kappa\)-action: \(\mathrm{IsScalarTower}\,\kappa\,\kappa[t_0, \dots,
1654	  t_{r-1}]\,M\). Indeed the algebra map \(\kappa \to \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)
1655	  followed by the polynomial action recovers the \(\kappa\)-action, since a scalar
1656	  \(c \in \kappa\) maps to the constant \(C\,c\), which acts as \(c\)
1657	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}).
1658	\end{lemma}
1659	
1660	\begin{definition}\leanok
1661	[Polynomial-ring submodule from a $t$-stable submodule]
1662	  \label{def:graded_polySubmodule}
1663	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polySubmodule}
1664	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyModule_X_smul,
1665	    lem:graded_polyModule_C_smul}
1666	  Let \(N \subseteq M\) be a \(\kappa\)-submodule that is stable under each \(t_i\)
1667	  (\(t_i \cdot N \subseteq N\)). Then \(N\), with the \emph{same} ambient carrier, is a
1668	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-submodule of \(M\) for the polynomial-module structure
1669	  (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}). Stability under the action of an arbitrary polynomial
1670	  follows by induction on monomials: constants act as scalars
1671	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyModule_C_smul}) and multiplication by \(X_i\) acts as \(t_i\)
1672	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyModule_X_smul}), both of which preserve \(N\).
1673	\end{definition}
1674	
1675	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1676	[The polynomial submodule has the same carrier]
1677	  \label{lem:graded_polySubmodule_coe}
1678	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polySubmodule_coe}
1679	  \uses{def:graded_polySubmodule}
1680	  The underlying set of \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}(t, N)\)
1681	  (\cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}) equals \(N\): the lift to a
1682	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-submodule does not change the carrier, so every object
1683	  in the induction remains an ambient submodule of the fixed \(M\).
1684	\end{lemma}
1685	
1686	\subsubsection*{Finiteness-transfer infrastructure}
1687	
1688	The ambient finiteness transfer (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer})
1689	factors the polynomial action of the larger ring through a surjection onto the
1690	smaller one. The following blocks build that surjection and the semilinearity
1691	identity it rests on, and record the two elementary finiteness manipulations
1692	(shrinking a numerator, enlarging a denominator) used to feed the kernel and
1693	cokernel constructors.
1694	
1695	\begin{definition}
1696	[The last-variable surjection of polynomial rings]
1697	  \label{lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom}
1698	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom}
1699	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_X_castSucc}
1700	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_X_last}
1701	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_C}
1702	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_rename_castSucc}
1703	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_surjective}
1704	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_ringHomSurjective}
1705	  \uses{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
1706	  For a field \(\kappa\) and \(r \in \mathbb{N}\), the \emph{last-variable
1707	  homomorphism} is the \(\kappa\)-algebra map
1708	  \[
1709	    \mathrm{lastVarAlgHom} : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]
1710	      = \operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0,\dots,r\}, \kappa)
1711	      \;\longrightarrow\;
1712	      \operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0,\dots,r-1\}, \kappa) = \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]
1713	  \]
1714	  obtained by evaluation: it sends the last variable \(X_r\) to \(0\) and each
1715	  earlier variable \(X_i\) (for \(0 \le i < r\)) to \(X_i\), and fixes constants.
1716	  It is a left inverse of the algebra map
1717	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \to \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)
1718	  induced by the canonical inclusion \(\{0, \dots, r-1\} \hookrightarrow \{0, \dots, r\}\)
1719	  on variable indices, and is therefore surjective. This is the concrete
1720	  ring surjection \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r] \twoheadrightarrow
1721	  \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) along which finiteness is transferred
1722	  (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}).
1723	\end{definition}
1724	
1725	\begin{lemma}
1726	[A $t$-stable submodule is closed under the polynomial action]
1727	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_mem_of_stable}
1728	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_mem_of_stable}
1729	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom, lem:graded_polyEndHom_X, lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1730	  Let \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\) be pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms
1731	  of \(M\) and let \(P' \subseteq M\) be a \(\kappa\)-submodule stable under each
1732	  \(t_i\) (\(t_i \cdot P' \subseteq P'\)). Then \(P'\) is closed under the action of
1733	  every polynomial: for all \(p \in \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) and \(m \in P'\),
1734	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(p)(m) \in P'\) (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}). The proof
1735	  is induction on \(p\): a constant acts as a scalar (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}),
1736	  multiplication by a variable acts as the stabilising \(t_i\)
1737	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}), and sums are handled additively.
1738	\end{lemma}
1739	
1740	\begin{lemma}
1741	[Mod-$P'$ semilinearity of the polynomial action]
1742	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem}
1743	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem}
1744	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom, lem:graded_polyEndHom_X, lem:graded_polyEndHom_C,
1745	    lem:graded_polyEndHom_mem_of_stable, lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom}
1746	  Let \(t_0, \dots, t_r\) be pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms of
1747	  \(M\), and let \(P' \le P\) be \(\kappa\)-submodules each stable under every
1748	  \(t_i\), with the last endomorphism \(x = t_r\) carrying \(P\) into \(P'\)
1749	  (\(x \cdot P \subseteq P'\)). Then for every polynomial
1750	  \(s \in \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\) and every \(m \in P\),
1751	  \[
1752	    \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(s)(m)
1753	      \;-\; \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1})
1754	        \bigl(\mathrm{lastVarAlgHom}(s)\bigr)(m)
1755	      \;\in\; P' .
1756	  \]
1757	  In words: acting by \(s\) through the full family \(t\) agrees, modulo \(P'\), with
1758	  first projecting away the last variable via \(\mathrm{lastVarAlgHom}\)
1759	  (\cref{lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom}) and acting through the reduced family
1760	  \((t_0, \dots, t_{r-1})\). This is the semilinearity identity that makes the
1761	  \(\sigma\)-semilinear quotient map of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}
1762	  well-defined. The proof is induction on \(s\): the case of an earlier variable
1763	  \(s = X_i\) (\(i < r\)) is the inductive hypothesis after \(\mathrm{lastVarAlgHom}\)
1764	  fixes it; the case of the last variable \(s = X_r\) annihilates the reduced term,
1765	  and the full term lands in \(P'\) because \(x = t_r\) carries \(P\) into the
1766	  \(t\)-stable \(P'\) (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_mem_of_stable}).
1767	\end{lemma}
1768	
1769	\begin{lemma}
1770	[Shrinking the numerator preserves finiteness]
1771	  \label{lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_numerator}
1772	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyQuot_finite_of_le_numerator}
1773	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1774	    lem:module_finite_of_injective_mathlib,
1775	    lem:mvPolynomial_isNoetherianRing_fin_mathlib,
1776	    lem:isNoetherian_of_isNoetherianRing_of_finite_mathlib}
1777	  Let \(N_1 \le N_2\) and \(P'\) be \(\kappa\)-submodules of \(M\), each stable under
1778	  the commuting family \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\), and suppose the subquotient
1779	  \(N_2/P'\) is finite over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)
1780	  (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}, \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}). Then \(N_1/P'\) is
1781	  also finite over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\). Indeed the inclusion
1782	  \(N_1 \subseteq N_2\) induces an injective \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-linear
1783	  map \(N_1/P' \hookrightarrow N_2/P'\); since
1784	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) is Noetherian
1785	  (\cref{lem:mvPolynomial_isNoetherianRing_fin_mathlib}), the finite module
1786	  \(N_2/P'\) is Noetherian
1787	  (\cref{lem:isNoetherian_of_isNoetherianRing_of_finite_mathlib}), so its submodule
1788	  \(N_1/P'\) is finite (\cref{lem:module_finite_of_injective_mathlib}). This supplies
1789	  the finiteness witness of the kernel constructor
1790	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker}).
1791	\end{lemma}
1792	
1793	\begin{lemma}
1794	[Enlarging the denominator preserves finiteness]
1795	  \label{lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_denominator}
1796	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyQuot_finite_of_le_denominator}
1797	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1798	    lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib}
1799	  Let \(N\) and \(P_1 \le P_2\) be \(\kappa\)-submodules of \(M\), each stable under
1800	  the commuting family \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\), and suppose the subquotient
1801	  \(N/P_1\) is finite over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)
1802	  (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}, \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}). Then \(N/P_2\) is
1803	  also finite over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\). Indeed the inclusion
1804	  \(P_1 \subseteq P_2\) gives a surjection \(N/P_1 \twoheadrightarrow N/P_2\)
1805	  (\cref{lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib}), and finiteness transfers along it
1806	  (\cref{lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib}). This supplies the
1807	  finiteness witness of the cokernel constructor
1808	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker}).
1809	\end{lemma}
1810	
1811	\subsubsection*{Ambient finiteness transfer}
1812	
1813	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1814	[Abstract single-pair finiteness transfer down one variable]
1815	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}
1816	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_finite_transfer}
1817	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1818	    lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom, lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem,
1819	    lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib,
1820	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
1821	  Let \(t_0, \dots, t_r\) be pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms of
1822	  \(M\), and let \(P' \le P\) be \(\kappa\)-submodules of \(M\), each stable under
1823	  every \(t_i\). Suppose the last endomorphism \(x = t_r\) carries \(P\) into \(P'\)
1824	  (\(x \cdot P \subseteq P'\)), and that the subquotient \(P/P'\) is finite over the
1825	  free polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\) for the polynomial-module structure
1826	  of \cref{def:graded_polyModule} (with carriers the ambient \(t\)-stable submodules
1827	  \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\), \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\),
1828	  \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}). Then \(P/P'\) is finite over
1829	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) for the reduced family \((t_0, \dots, t_{r-1})\).
1830	
1831	  This is the abstract single-pair transfer: it does not mention the kernel/cokernel
1832	  pairs, but is the engine applied to each of them by the kernel and cokernel
1833	  constructors (\cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker},
1834	  \cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker}). Because the last variable
1835	  annihilates the subquotient, the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-action factors through
1836	  the last-variable surjection \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r] \twoheadrightarrow
1837	  \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), which is what makes the transfer drop one variable.
1838	\end{lemma}
1839	
1840	\begin{proof}
1841	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1842	    lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom, lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem,
1843	    lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib}
1844	  Write \(\sigma = \mathrm{lastVarAlgHom} : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]
1845	  \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) for the surjective ring
1846	  homomorphism sending the last variable to \(0\) and each earlier variable \(X_i\)
1847	  (\(i < r\)) to \(X_i\) (\cref{lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom}). Consider the map on numerators
1848	  \[
1849	    g : \mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P \;\longrightarrow\;
1850	      \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\bigr)\big/
1851	        \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\bigr), \qquad y \longmapsto [\,y\,],
1852	  \]
1853	  where the source carries the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-module structure and the
1854	  target the reduced \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-module structure (both with the
1855	  same ambient carrier \(P\), \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}). The mod-\(P'\)
1856	  semilinearity identity (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem}) -- acting by
1857	  \(s\) through the full family and acting by \(\sigma(s)\) through the reduced family
1858	  agree modulo \(P'\), precisely because \(x = t_r\) carries \(P\) into \(P'\) --
1859	  says exactly that \(g\) is \(\sigma\)-semilinear. The map \(g\) is surjective (it is
1860	  the quotient projection on the common carrier), and it kills the denominator
1861	  \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\), so it descends through that submodule
1862	  (\cref{lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib}) to a surjective
1863	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-linear map
1864	  \[
1865	    \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\bigr)\big/\bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\bigr)
1866	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\;
1867	    \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\bigr)\big/\bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\bigr)
1868	  \]
1869	  from the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-finite subquotient onto the reduced one.
1870	  Finiteness transfers along this surjection
1871	  (\cref{lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib}), giving finiteness of \(P/P'\)
1872	  over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\).
1873	
1874	  \emph{Why the free polynomial ring.} The smaller ring must be the \emph{free}
1875	  polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), not the subalgebra
1876	  \(\mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\} \subseteq
1877	  \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\): relations among the endomorphisms inside
1878	  \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) would obstruct the polynomial-ring surjection
1879	  \(\sigma\) that the scalar transfer requires
1880	  (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}). Keeping free polynomial
1881	  rings at every step keeps \(\sigma\) -- and hence the transfer -- available
1882	  throughout. Everything is finite generation of ambient submodules of the fixed
1883	  \(M\) and their quotients.
1884	\end{proof}
1885	
1886	\subsubsection*{Subquotient constructors}
1887	
1888	The kernel/cokernel closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}) and the
1889	abstract finiteness transfer (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) are
1890	bundled into two constructors that take a length-\((r+1)\) subquotient datum to the
1891	length-\(r\) kernel and cokernel data consumed by the induction. The two stability
1892	lemmas below first record that the new pairs are stable under the \emph{whole}
1893	endomorphism family (not merely the reduced one), which is what lets the over-\(\kappa[t_0,\dots,t_r]\)
1894	finiteness inputs be stated before the variable is dropped.
1895	
1896	\begin{lemma}
1897	[The kernel pair's lower module is stable under the full family]
1898	  \label{lem:graded_ker_stable_full}
1899	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_stable_full}
1900	  \uses{lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute}
1901	  Let \((N, N')\) be a length-\((r+1)\) subquotient datum
1902	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_r\) and
1903	  \(x = t_r\). Then the kernel pair's lower module \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is stable
1904	  under \emph{every} \(t_i\) (\(0 \le i \le r\)), not only the reduced family: each
1905	  \(t_i\) preserves \(N\) and preserves the preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) because it
1906	  commutes with \(x\) and preserves \(N'\)

1	\chapter{Regrouping isomorphism for the affine base-change tensor tower}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_RegroupHelper}

11	\section{The R'-linear regrouping equivalence}
12	
13	\begin{lemma}
14	
15	\leanok
16	[Regrouping linear equivalence for the tensor tower]
17	  \label{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}
18	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}
19	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
20	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
21	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
22	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
23	  % L933--938).
24	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
25	  % equality
26	  % $$
27	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
28	  % $$
29	  % as $R'$-modules."
30	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
31	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
32	  Let \(\psi : R \to R'\) and \(\varphi : R \to A\) be ring homomorphisms and \(M\)
33	  an \(A\)-module. On the \emph{canonical} Mathlib instances (with \(R'\) acting on
34	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) through the \(\texttt{Algebra.TensorProduct.rightAlgebra}\)
35	  factor, i.e.\ \(r' \cdot (a \otimes s) = a \otimes (r' s)\)) there is a bundled
36	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism
37	  \[
38	    \operatorname{regroup} :
39	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M ,
40	  \]
41	  the ``equality as \(R'\)-modules'' of the affine base-change source. On generators
42	  it sends \((a \otimes r') \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes (a \cdot m)\); in
43	  particular \((1 \otimes r') \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes m\), with inverse
44	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\). It carries \emph{no} flatness
45	  hypothesis.
46	\end{lemma}
47	
48	\begin{proof}
49	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
50	  \leanok
51	  This is pure tensor algebra, and it is supplied \emph{directly} by Mathlib's
52	  pushout-form base-change cancellation
53	  \(\operatorname{Algebra.IsPushout.cancelBaseChange}\)
54	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}), instantiated at \(S = R'\),
55	  \(B = A \otimes_R R'\):
56	  \[
57	    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} R\,R'\,A\,(A \otimes_R R')\,M :
58	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M .
59	  \]
60	  The required pushout instance \(\operatorname{Algebra.IsPushout} R\,R'\,A\,
61	  (A \otimes_R R')\) is supplied for free by \(\operatorname{TensorProduct.isPushout'}\).
62	  The decisive structural point is that this equivalence is \emph{natively}
63	  \(R'\)-linear: the cancellation is bundled as a \(\operatorname{LinearEquiv}\) over
64	  \(R'\) (its base-ring slot is \(S = R'\)), so the \(R'\)-linearity is part of the
65	  Mathlib datum with no hand-written scalar-compatibility obligation.
66	  On the generator it computes, by \(\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_tmul}\),
67	  \[
68	    (a \otimes r') \otimes m \;\longmapsto\; r' \otimes (a \cdot m) ,
69	  \]
70	  with inverse \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\)
71	  (\(\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_symm\_tmul}\)); the \(R'\)-action enters through the
72	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) factor (\(r' \cdot (a \otimes s) = a \otimes (r' s)\)). Being a
73	  single bundled Mathlib linear equivalence it is an \(R'\)-linear isomorphism, and it
74	  carries no flatness hypothesis.
75	
76	\end{proof}
77

976	  Let \(R \to S\) and \(R \to T\) be \(R\)-algebras. Then the fibre product of the
977	  induced morphisms \(\operatorname{Spec}(S) \to \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) and
978	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(T) \to \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) is canonically the spectrum of
979	  the tensor product,
980	  \[
981	    \operatorname{Limits.pullback}\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}(R\to S),\,
982	      \operatorname{Spec}(R\to T)\bigr)
983	    \;\cong\; \operatorname{Spec}\bigl(S \otimes_R T\bigr)
984	    \qquad (\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackSpecIso}),
985	  \]
986	  and under this isomorphism the two cone legs are the \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-maps of
987	  the canonical tensor inclusions: \texttt{pullbackSpecIso\_hom\_fst} identifies the
988	  first projection with \(\operatorname{Spec}\) of
989	  \(\texttt{Algebra.TensorProduct.includeLeftRingHom} : S \to S \otimes_R T\), and
990	  \texttt{pullbackSpecIso\_hom\_snd} identifies the second projection with
991	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\) of
992	  \(\texttt{Algebra.TensorProduct.includeRight} : T \to S \otimes_R T\) (with companion
993	  forms \texttt{pullbackSpecIso\_inv\_fst}, \texttt{\_inv\_snd}).
994	\end{lemma}

996	\begin{lemma}[Cancellation of base change for the tensor tower]
997	  \label{lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
998	  \lean{TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.cancelBaseChange}
999	  \mathlibok
1000	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1001	  Let \(R \to A \to B\) be a tower of algebras, \(M\) a \(B\)-module that is also an
1002	  \(A\)- and \(R\)-module compatibly, and \(N\) an \(R\)-module. Then there is a
1003	  \(B\)-linear equivalence, carrying \emph{no} flatness hypothesis,
1004	  \[
1005	    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} :
1006	    M \otimes_A (A \otimes_R N) \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; M \otimes_R N,
1007	  \]
1008	  with \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\bigl(m \otimes_A (a \otimes_R n)\bigr)
1009	  = (a \cdot m) \otimes_R n\) (\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_tmul}) and inverse
1010	  \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}^{-1}(m \otimes_R n) = m \otimes_A (1 \otimes_R n)\)
1011	  (\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_symm\_tmul}). Instantiated at the affine--affine model
1012	  (\(M = R'\), \(B = R'\), \(N = M\)) this is the cancellation isomorphism
1013	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \cong R' \otimes_R M\) used below, read in either
1014	  orientation.
1015	\end{lemma}

1017	\begin{lemma}[Pushout cancellation of base change (natively \(R'\)-linear)]
1018	  \label{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
1019	  \lean{Algebra.IsPushout.cancelBaseChange}
1020	  \mathlibok
1021	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1022	  Let \(R \to S\) and \(R \to A\) be commutative-ring maps and let \(B\) be an
1023	  \(A\)-algebra and \(S\)-algebra making the square \(R \to S,\ R \to A,\ A \to B,\
1024	  S \to B\) a pushout of commutative rings, i.e.\ \([\operatorname{Algebra.IsPushout} R S
1025	  A B]\). Then for any \(A\)-module \(M\) there is a bundled \(S\)-linear equivalence
1026	  \[
1027	    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} R\,S\,A\,B\,M :
1028	    B \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; S \otimes_R M ,
1029	  \]
1030	  with \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) and its inverse computed on elementary tensors
1031	  by \texttt{cancelBaseChange\_tmul} / \texttt{cancelBaseChange\_symm\_tmul}. The
1032	  \(S\)-linearity is part of the bundled \(\operatorname{LinearEquiv}\): there is
1033	  \emph{no} separate \texttt{map\_smul'} obligation to discharge. Instantiated at
1034	  \(S = R'\), \(B = A \otimes_R R'\) — for which the pushout instance
1035	  \(\operatorname{Algebra.IsPushout} R\,R'\,A\,(A \otimes_R R')\) is supplied for free by
1036	  \(\operatorname{TensorProduct.isPushout'}\) — it is the natively \(R'\)-linear
1037	  cancellation \((A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \cong R' \otimes_R M\) used as the core of
1038	  the regrouping equivalence below.
1039	\end{lemma}

1080	  \uses{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso, lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}

1084	  % step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L927--938).

1086	  % to describe pullbacks and pushforwards of $\mathcal{F}$."
1087	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
1088	  change'' (proof, ``describe pullbacks and pushforwards'' step).}
1089	  In the affine--affine model (\(g = \operatorname{Spec}\psi\),
1090	  \(f = \operatorname{Spec}\varphi\), \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde M\) for an
1091	  \(A\)-module \(M\)), the global sections of the domain \(g^*(f_*\widetilde M)\) of the
1092	  base-change map are canonically \(R' \otimes_R M\) as an \(R'\)-module. Explicitly,
1093	  the assembled isomorphism
1094	  \[
1095	    \Theta_{\mathrm{src}} :
1096	    \Gamma\bigl(g^*(f_*\widetilde M)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } R' \otimes_R M
1097	  \]
1098	  is the composite of the pushforward dictionary
1099	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso} (reading \(f_*\widetilde M\) as
1100	  restriction of scalars \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\)) followed by the pullback
1101	  dictionary Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} (reading \(g^*\) of a tilde as
1102	  extension of scalars \(R' \otimes_R (-)\) along \(\psi\)).
1103	\end{lemma}
1104	
1105	\begin{proof}
1106	  \uses{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso, lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}
1107	  The domain \(g^*(f_*\widetilde M)\) involves only the cospan maps
1108	  \(f = \operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) and \(g = \operatorname{Spec}\psi\), each a genuine
1109	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-map, so the two affine dictionaries apply directly with no
1110	  pullback-leg identification needed. The pushforward dictionary
1111	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso} gives
1112	  \(f_*\widetilde M = \widetilde{\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M}\) on global sections,
1113	  i.e.\ \(M\) viewed as an \(R\)-module; the pullback dictionary
1114	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} gives
1115	  \(g^*\widetilde N = \widetilde{R' \otimes_R N}\), so
1116	  \(\Gamma\bigl(g^*(f_*\widetilde M)\bigr) = R' \otimes_R M\) as an \(R'\)-module.
1117	  Composing the two dictionary isomorphisms in this order is \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}\).
1118	\end{proof}

1242	% --- Supporting lemmas for the codomain read ---
1243	% These helpers establish that pullback along a scheme isomorphism is an equivalence
1244	% of module categories; the adjunction unit of an equivalence is an isomorphism,
1245	% used in the codomain-read computation.
1246	
1247	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1248	[Pullback along an isomorphism is an equivalence of module categories]
1249	  \label{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1250	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1251	  Let \(f : X \to Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes. Then the pullback functor on
1252	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is
1253	  one half of an equivalence of categories
1254	  \(Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \simeq X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\), with quasi-inverse
1255	  \((f^{-1})^*\).
1256	\end{lemma}
1257	\begin{proof}
1258	
1259	  Assemble the equivalence from the functor \(f^*\) and the candidate inverse
1260	  \((f^{-1})^*\). The unit and counit isomorphisms are built from the pseudofunctor
1261	  coherences of pullback: \((f^{-1})^* \circ f^* \cong (f \circ f^{-1})^* = \mathrm{id}^*
1262	  \cong \mathrm{id}\) and symmetrically, using
1263	  \(f \circ f^{-1} = \mathrm{id}_Y\), \(f^{-1} \circ f = \mathrm{id}_X\)
1264	  (composition-of-pullbacks, congruence under equal maps, and pullback-of-identity).
1265	  The triangle identities are forced by these coherences.
1266	\end{proof}
1267	
1268	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1269	[The pullback functor along an isomorphism is an equivalence]
1270	  \label{lem:pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
1271	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
1272	  \uses{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1273	  Consequently, for an isomorphism \(f : X \to Y\) the functor
1274	  \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is an equivalence of
1275	  categories. In particular its adjunction unit is a natural isomorphism, so
1276	  \((\eta_{f})_{W}\) is invertible on every object \(W\).
1277	\end{lemma}
1278	\begin{proof}
1279	
1280	  The functor underlying the equivalence of
1281	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso} is exactly \(f^*\), so \(f^*\) is an
1282	  equivalence; an equivalence's unit is a natural isomorphism.
1283	\end{proof}

1287	%     R'-base change of the algebraic unit) ← IsIso corollary (the pinned leaf). ---

1289	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1290	[Regrouping isomorphism for the section-level mate]
1291	  \label{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}
1292	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}
1293	  % NOTE: tensor-order convention. The Lean uses Mathlib's `pullbackSpecIso`
1294	  % orientation `A ⊗[R] R'` (so the codomain is `(A ⊗_R R') ⊗_A M` and the generator
1295	  % goes `r' ⊗ m ↦ (1 ⊗ r') ⊗ m`); the prose below writes `R' ⊗_R A`. The two are the
1296	  % same module up to `TensorProduct.comm` — a faithful re-orientation, no content change.
1297	  \uses{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv, lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
1298	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
1299	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
1300	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
1301	  % L933--938).
1302	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
1303	  % equality
1304	  % $$
1305	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
1306	  % $$
1307	  % as $R'$-modules."
1308	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
1309	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
1310	  Let \(\psi : R \to R'\) and \(\varphi : R \to A\) be ring homomorphisms and \(M\) an
1311	  \(A\)-module. There is a bundled \(R'\)-linear isomorphism
1312	  \[
1313	    \operatorname{regroup} :
1314	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M ,
1315	  \]
1316	  the ``equality as \(R'\)-modules'' of the source, sending the generator
1317	  \((r' \otimes 1) \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes m\); equivalently its inverse sends
1318	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\). It carries \emph{no} flatness
1319	  hypothesis.
1320	\end{lemma}
1321	
1322	\begin{proof}
1323	  \uses{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv, lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
1324	  The underlying \(R'\)-linear equivalence is supplied, already as a bundled
1325	  \(\operatorname{LinearEquiv}\) over \(R'\), by the standalone geometry-free helper
1326	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}, whose construction is now the
1327	  natively \(R'\)-linear pushout-cancellation equivalence of
1328	  Lemma~\ref{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}. Crucially, that helper bundles
1329	  \(R'\)-linearity \emph{internally}: there is no hand-written scalar-compatibility
1330	  obligation, no induction over elementary tensors, and in particular \emph{no} residual
1331	  \(r' \cdot 0 = 0\) bookkeeping branch. The only work left to this proof is therefore to
1332	  promote that \(R'\)-linear equivalence to a morphism of \(R'\)-module \emph{objects} in
1333	  the exact extension/restriction-of-scalars packaging that the codomain and domain reads
1334	  produce. We work in the \(A \otimes_R R'\) orientation
1335	  (the same module as \(R' \otimes_R A\) up to the canonical commutativity isomorphism,
1336	  per the tensor-order convention note).
1337	
1338	  \emph{The lone residual obstruction is the \(A\)-action diamond, resolved at the object
1339	  level.} The pushout-cancellation helper takes its relative tensor product over the
1340	  \emph{canonical} \(A\)-algebra structure on \(A \otimes_R R'\)
1341	  (multiplication in the left factor), whereas the carrier object produced by the
1342	  dictionaries,
1343	  the extension of scalars of \(M\) along the left inclusion \(\iota : A \to A \otimes_R R'\),
1344	  tensors over the \(A\)-module structure on \(A \otimes_R R'\) induced by \(\iota\). These
1345	  two \(A\)-module structures on
1346	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) agree on elements but are distinct as data, so the two relative
1347	  tensor products are distinct types even though their underlying abelian groups and
1348	  their \(R'\)-actions coincide. This diamond is the \emph{only} thing the helper does
1349	  not already discharge, and — because the \(R'\)-linearity is now entirely internal to
1350	  the Mathlib equivalence — it is resolved purely at the carrier level by an
1351	  identity-on-elements \(R'\)-linear bridge
1352	  \[
1353	    eT : (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\operatorname{restr}} M
1354	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1355	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\mathrm{can}} M ,
1356	  \]
1357	  the identity map on generators, which is \(R'\)-linear because both sides carry the
1358	  same \(R'\)-action (restriction of scalars along \(R' \to A \otimes_R R'\)).
1359	
1360	  Composing the bridge \(eT\) with the natively \(R'\)-linear helper equivalence and
1361	  promoting to an isomorphism of module objects yields the desired bundled
1362	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism of \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\) objects. Being a composite
1363	  of linear equivalences it is an \(R'\)-linear isomorphism, and none of its constituents
1364	  carries a flatness hypothesis.
1365	\end{proof}

1457	\begin{lemma}[Unit-across-conjugate coherence]

1461	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1462	  Let \(\mathrm{adj}_1 : L_1 \dashv R_1\) and \(\mathrm{adj}_2 : L_2 \dashv R_2\) be two
1463	  adjunctions between the same pair of categories, with units \(\eta_1\) and \(\eta_2\), and let

1510	\begin{lemma}
1511	
1512	\leanok
1513	[Pseudofunctoriality of the pullback--pushforward unit]
1514	  \label{lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}
1515	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}
1516	  \uses{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib, lem:comp_unit_app_mathlib,
1517	        lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib}
1518	  Let \(a : X_1 \to X_2\) and \(b : X_2 \to X_3\) be composable morphisms of schemes and let
1519	  \(N\) be an \(\mathcal{O}_{X_3}\)-module. Write \(\eta^{h}\) for the unit of the
1520	  pullback--pushforward adjunction \((h^{*}\dashv h_{*})\) attached to a morphism \(h\), and
1521	  \(\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b}\), \(\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b}\) for the pseudofunctor
1522	  composition coherences. Then
1523	  \[
1524	    \eta^{b}_{N} \;\circ\; b_{*}\bigl(\eta^{a}_{\,b^{*}N}\bigr)
1525	      \;\circ\; (\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{hom}}_{N}
1526	    \;=\;
1527	    \eta^{a\circ b}_{N} \;\circ\;
1528	      (a\circ b)_{*}\bigl((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}_{N}\bigr),
1529	  \]
1530	  where composites are read left to right. In words: the unit of the composite morphism
1531	  \(a \circ b\), reindexed by the inverse pullback coherence, factors as the \(b\)-unit, followed

1541	  composite adjunction \((b^{*}\dashv b_{*}) . (a^{*}\dashv a_{*})\), the adjunction
1542	  \(\bigl((a\circ b)^{*}\dashv (a\circ b)_{*}\bigr)\), and the left-adjoint transformation
1543	  \((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}\). This yields an identity whose right-hand factor
1544	  is the conjugate (mate) of \((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}\); by
1545	  Lemma~\ref{lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib} that mate is exactly the pushforward

1922	  t_r]\) finiteness input of the cokernel constructor.
1923	\end{lemma}
1924	
1925	\begin{definition}
1926	[Kernel constructor of a subquotient datum]
1927	  \label{def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker}
1928	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum.ker}
1929	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1930	    lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_ker_le, lem:graded_ker_annihilate,
1931	    lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute, lem:graded_ker_stable_full,
1932	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer,
1933	    lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_numerator}
1934	  From a length-\((r+1)\) subquotient datum \((N, N')\) with endomorphisms
1935	  \(t_0, \dots, t_r\) and \(x = t_r\) (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}), the
1936	  \emph{kernel constructor} produces the length-\(r\) datum
1937	  \[
1938	    \ker D \;=\; \bigl(\,N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\ N'\,\bigr)
1939	  \]
1940	  on the reduced family \((t_0, \dots, t_{r-1})\). Its
1941	  non-finiteness fields are supplied by the ambient kernel calculus: homogeneity of
1942	  \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous}), the nesting
1943	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_ker_le}), and stability under the
1944	  reduced family (\cref{lem:graded_ker_stable_full}). The finiteness witness
1945	  is obtained by first shrinking the numerator from \(N\) to
1946	  \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_numerator}) to get
1947	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-finiteness of the kernel subquotient, then transferring
1948	  down one variable (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}), valid because
1949	  \(x\) annihilates the kernel subquotient (\cref{lem:graded_ker_annihilate}). This
1950	  realises the kernel half of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker} as a genuine
1951	  length-\(r\) datum.
1952	\end{definition}
1953	
1954	\begin{definition}
1955	[Cokernel constructor of a subquotient datum]
1956	  \label{def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker}
1957	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum.coker}
1958	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1959	    lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_coker_annihilate,
1960	    lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute, lem:graded_coker_stable_full,
1961	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer,
1962	    lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_denominator}
1963	  From the same length-\((r+1)\) datum, the \emph{cokernel constructor} produces the
1964	  length-\(r\) datum
1965	  \[
1966	    \operatorname{coker} D \;=\; \bigl(\,N,\ N' + x \cdot N\,\bigr)
1967	  \]
1968	  on the reduced family. Its non-finiteness fields come from the ambient cokernel
1969	  calculus: homogeneity of \(N' + x \cdot N\) (\cref{lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous}),
1970	  the nesting \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\) (\cref{lem:graded_coker_le}), and stability
1971	  under the reduced family (\cref{lem:graded_coker_stable_full}). The finiteness field
1972	  is obtained by enlarging the denominator from \(N'\) to \(N' + x \cdot N\)
1973	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_denominator}) to get
1974	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-finiteness of the cokernel subquotient, then
1975	  transferring down one variable (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}),
1976	  valid because \(x\) annihilates the cokernel subquotient
1977	  (\cref{lem:graded_coker_annihilate}). This realises the cokernel half of
1978	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker} as a genuine length-\(r\) datum.
1979	\end{definition}

1981	\subsubsection*{The degreewise difference identity}
1982	
1983	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1984	[$D_5$ -- degreewise rank--nullity difference]
1985	  \label{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}
1986	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.degreewise_finrank_diff}
1987	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
1988	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
1989	  Let \(\varphi : V \to W\) be a \(\kappa\)-linear map between finite-dimensional
1990	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. Then
1991	  \[
1992	    \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V
1993	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi).
1994	  \]
1995	  Applied degreewise with \(\varphi = (x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\),
1996	  \(V = \mathcal{M}_n\), \(W = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) -- so that \(\ker\varphi\) is the
1997	  degree-\(n\) piece \(K_n\) of the kernel subquotient and
1998	  \(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x \mathcal{M}_n\) the
1999	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(C_{n+1}\) of the cokernel subquotient
2000	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, via the split \(G_1\)
2001	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq} and the degree shift
2002	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) -- this gives, for every \(n\),
2003	  \[
2004	    \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_n
2005	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa C_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa K_n,
2006	  \]
2007	  which is the difference identity \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) consumed by
2008	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}. No graded structure is used in proving the
2009	  underlying vector-space identity -- it is pure rank--nullity.
2010	\end{lemma}
2011	
2012	\begin{proof}
2013	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
2014	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
2015	  By rank--nullity (\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}),
2016	  \(\dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}
2017	  \varphi)\), so \(\dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa V -
2018	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). By additivity on the short exact sequence
2019	  \(0 \to \operatorname{range}\varphi \to W \to W/\operatorname{range}\varphi \to 0\)
2020	  (\cref{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}),
2021	  \(\dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W -
2022	  \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V +
2023	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). Rearranging gives
2024	  \(\dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) -
2025	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). The degreewise application identifies
2026	  \(\ker(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\) and
2027	  \(\mathcal{M}_{n+1}/\operatorname{range}(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\)
2028	  with the degree-\(n\) piece of the kernel subquotient and the degree-\((n+1)\)
2029	  piece of the cokernel subquotient (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
2030	  the latter because the degree-\((n+1)\) graded piece of the homogeneous submodule
2031	  \(x\mathcal{M}\) is exactly \(x \mathcal{M}_n\)
2032	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}).
2033	\end{proof}
2034	
2035	\subsubsection*{The base case ($r = 0$)}
2036	
2037	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2038	[Base-case finiteness over the empty polynomial ring]
2039	  \label{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2040	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2041	  Let \(Q\) be a \(\kappa\)-vector space that is also a module over
2042	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa)\) for an \emph{empty} index set
2043	  \(\sigma\) -- in particular \(\sigma = \{0, \dots, r-1\}\) with \(r = 0\) -- in a way
2044	  compatible with the \(\kappa\)-action (scalar tower). If \(Q\) is module-finite over
2045	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa)\), then \(Q\) is finite-dimensional over
2046	  \(\kappa\). Indeed for empty \(\sigma\) the polynomial ring is
2047	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa) \cong \kappa\), a finite \(\kappa\)-module,
2048	  so finiteness of \(Q\) over it transfers to finiteness over \(\kappa\) along the scalar
2049	  tower. This is the length-zero input to the ambient subquotient induction
2050	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}): when \(r = 0\) the subquotient \(N/N'\) is a
2051	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space, hence \(\mathrm{hilb}\) is eventually zero.
2052	\end{lemma}
2053	
2054	\begin{lemma}
2055	[Base case: the length-zero subquotient Hilbert function vanishes eventually]
2056	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero}
2057	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_base_eventuallyZero}
2058	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb,
2059	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite,
2060	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
2061	    lem:finite_ne_bot_of_iSupIndep_mathlib, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
2062	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
2063	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
2064	  % 00K1), proof, base case (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex,
2065	  % L13908-L13910).
2066	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2067	  % "We prove this by induction on the minimal number of
2068	  %  generators of $S_1$. If this number is $0$, then
2069	  %  $M_n = 0$ for all $n \gg 0$ and the result holds."
2070	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (base case of the induction).}
2071	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum of length \(0\)
2072	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}): a homogeneous pair \(N' \le N\) of the fixed
2073	  ambient graded module \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) with \emph{no} endomorphisms,
2074	  whose finiteness witness says the subquotient \(Q_0 := N/N'\) is module-finite over
2075	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\varnothing, \kappa)\). Then the ambient Hilbert
2076	  function vanishes eventually: there is \(K \in \mathbb{N}\) with
2077	  \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = 0\) for all \(n > K\). Consequently
2078	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\) (\cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}),
2079	  which is the base case of the ambient subquotient induction
2080	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}).
2081	\end{lemma}
2082	
2083	\begin{proof}
2084	  \uses{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite,
2085	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
2086	    lem:finite_ne_bot_of_iSupIndep_mathlib, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
2087	  Since the index set of variables is empty,
2088	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\varnothing, \kappa) \cong \kappa\), so the finiteness
2089	  witness makes the subquotient \(Q_0 = N/N'\) a \emph{finite-dimensional}
2090	  \(\kappa\)-vector space
2091	  (\cref{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}). For each
2092	  degree \(n\), composing the inclusion of the homogeneous piece
2093	  \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n \hookrightarrow N\) with the quotient projection
2094	  \(N \twoheadrightarrow Q_0\) gives a \(\kappa\)-linear map
2095	  \(\psi_n : N \cap \mathcal{M}_n \to Q_0\), \(m \mapsto [m]\), whose image is the
2096	  class of the degree-\(n\) homogeneous piece inside the subquotient.
2097	
2098	  \emph{Step 1: independence of the images.} The family
2099	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n)\bigr)_{n}\) is \(\mathrm{iSupIndep}\) in
2100	  \(Q_0\). The reason is the ambient direct-sum grading of \(M\): the homogeneous
2101	  pieces \(\mathcal{M}_n\) form an independent family
2102	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}). An element of
2103	  \(\bigsqcup_{j \ne n} \operatorname{range}(\psi_j)\) is a finite sum of classes of
2104	  homogeneous elements of degrees \(j \ne n\); since \(N'\) is homogeneous, the
2105	  degree-\(n\) homogeneous component of any representative of such a class lies in
2106	  \(N'\), so the class has zero degree-\(n\) component in \(Q_0\). A nonzero class in
2107	  \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n)\), by contrast, is represented by a homogeneous
2108	  element of degree \(n\) not in \(N'\), so has nonzero degree-\(n\) component.
2109	  Comparing degree-\(n\) components forces the intersection
2110	  \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n) \cap \bigsqcup_{j \ne n} \operatorname{range}(\psi_j)
2111	  = 0\); independence follows.
2112	  % NOTE: ROUTE (b). Prove the iSupIndep by direct membership analysis in
2113	  %   ⨆_{j ≠ n} range(ψ j), reading off the degree-n homogeneous component
2114	  %   directly inside Q₀'s fixed κ-module structure.
2115	  %   No outgoing linear map out of Q₀ is constructed, so no scalar ring other than κ
2116	  %   is ever introduced.
2117	  % NOTE: ROUTE (a). Building a κ-linear detector
2118	  %   Φ : Q₀ → M/N', [m] ↦ [π_n m] (degree-n projection) via descent through the
2119	  %   subquotient fails: the descended map carries the scalar action of Q₀'s base
2120	  %   ring, but the target M/N' carries only a κ-module structure -- a scalar-ring
2121	  %   incompatibility. Route (b) avoids this by working entirely within Q₀.
2122	
2123	  \emph{Step 2: finiteness of the support.} Because \(Q_0\) is finite-dimensional and
2124	  the ranges \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n)\) are independent, only finitely many of
2125	  them are nonzero: the set \(\{\,n : \operatorname{range}(\psi_n) \ne 0\,\}\) is
2126	  finite (\cref{lem:finite_ne_bot_of_iSupIndep_mathlib}). In particular it is bounded
2127	  above, by some \(K \in \mathbb{N}\).
2128	
2129	  \emph{Step 3: eventual vanishing.} For \(n > K\) we have
2130	  \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n) = 0\), i.e.\ every homogeneous element of
2131	  \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\) has class \(0\) in \(Q_0 = N/N'\); equivalently
2132	  \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n \le N'\), hence \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n = N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n\)
2133	  and
2134	  \[
2135	    \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; \dim_\kappa(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
2136	      - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n) \;=\; 0 .
2137	  \]
2138	  Thus \(\mathrm{hilb}\) is eventually zero, and
2139	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero} gives \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\).
2140	\end{proof}
2141	
2142	\subsubsection*{The ambient subquotient induction}
2143	
2144	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2145	[Rationality of the ambient subquotient Hilbert function]
2146	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}
2147	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_hilbertSeries_rational}
2148	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
2149	    def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker, def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker,
2150	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
2151	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero,
2152	    lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq, lem:ratHilb_bump,
2153	    lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero,
2154	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2155	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
2156	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
2157	  % 00K1), proof (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13908-L13914).
2158	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (induction on the number of
2159	  degree-one generators).}
2160	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum of length \(r\)
2161	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with ambient Hilbert function
2162	  \(\mathrm{hilb}\). Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, r)\)
2163	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}): the difference \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
2164	  - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), the coefficient
2165	  sequence of a rational series \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-r}\) with \(p \in
2166	  \mathbb{Q}[X]\). The induction is on the length \(r\), and every object that
2167	  appears is an ambient pair of homogeneous submodules of the fixed graded module
2168	  \(M\).
2169	\end{lemma}
2170	
2171	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, induction set-up (read
2172	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13908-L13914).
2173	% "We prove this by induction on the minimal number of
2174	%  generators of $S_1$. If this number is $0$, then
2175	%  $M_n = 0$ for all $n \gg 0$ and the result holds.
2176	%  To prove the induction step, let $x\in S_1$
2177	%  be one of a minimal set of generators, such that
2178	%  the induction hypothesis applies to the
2179	%  graded ring $S/(x)$."
2180	\begin{proof}
2181	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
2182	    def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker, def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker,
2183	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
2184	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero,
2185	    lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq, lem:ratHilb_bump,
2186	    lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero,
2187	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2188	  We induct on the length \(r\).
2189	
2190	  \emph{Base case \(r = 0\).} A length-zero datum has a finiteness witness making the
2191	  subquotient \(N/N'\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space
2192	  (\cref{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}); its degreewise
2193	  images form an independent family inside that finite-dimensional space, so only
2194	  finitely many are nonzero and \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = 0\) for \(n \gg 0\)
2195	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero}). By
2196	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero} an eventually-zero function is rational of order
2197	  \(0\), giving \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\).
2198	
2199	  \emph{Inductive step \(r \ge 1\).} Set \(x = t_{r-1}\). By the kernel/cokernel
2200	  closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}) the kernel subquotient
2201	  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and the cokernel subquotient
2202	  \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) are subquotient data of length \(r-1\), assembled by
2203	  the kernel and cokernel constructors (\cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker},
2204	  \cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker}) whose finiteness witnesses come from the
2205	  abstract finiteness transfer (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}). The
2206	  induction hypothesis applied
2207	  to \(K\) and \(C\) gives \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and
2208	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}). By the degreewise
2209	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
2210	  \[
2211	    \mathrm{hilb}(n+1) - \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)
2212	    \qquad \text{for all } n .
2213	  \]
2214	  Feeding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\), \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and this
2215	  difference identity into the inductive-step engine
2216	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}) yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, r)\). (The
2217	  common order \(r-1\) for \(h_C\) and \(h_K\) is arranged, if necessary, by raising
2218	  the lower one with \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump}.) This completes the induction.
2219	\end{proof}
