2221	\section{Support and freeness predicates}
2222	\label{sec:quot_predicates}
2223	
2224	The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
2225	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
2226	geometric conditions: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support
2227	proper over the base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally
2228	free of a fixed rank}. This section introduces both predicates.
2229	
2230	\begin{definition}
2231	
2232	\leanok
2233	[Annihilator ideal sheaf of a sheaf of modules]
2234	  \label{def:modules_annihilator}
2235	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator}
2236	  \uses{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
2237	  % NOTE: The definition itself is formalized and axiom-clean; the inclusion direction
2238	  % \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U) \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\)
2239	  % (\cref{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}) is closed. The reverse inclusion, which identifies
2240	  % the annihilator sheaf with the module-annihilator on affine opens, remains bridge-gated on
2241	  % \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}.
2242	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
2243	  % Polynomials" (read from
2244	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
2245	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2246	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
2247	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
2248	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
2249	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
2250	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
2251	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of
2252	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is \emph{quasi-coherent and of finite type}
2253	  (over a locally noetherian base, so that on each affine open \(U\) the section
2254	  module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is a finitely generated \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module).
2255	  The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ideal sheaf
2256	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) defined on affine opens: for each affine open
2257	  \(U \subseteq X\),
2258	  \[
2259	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
2260	    \;=\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr)
2261	    \;\subseteq\; \mathcal{O}_X(U),
2262	  \]
2263	  the annihilator of the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\). These
2264	  local data patch to a coherent ideal sheaf on \(X\) whose vanishing locus is
2265	  the set-theoretic support of \(\mathcal{F}\).
2266	
2267	  \emph{Well-definedness on basic opens.} The coherence condition for an ideal
2268	  sheaf requires that, for an affine open \(U\) and \(f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
2269	  the section \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)\) restricts correctly to the basic
2270	  open \(D(f) \subseteq U\), i.e. that
2271	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(D(f))}(\mathcal{F}(D(f)))\) is the extension of
2272	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\) along the localization map
2273	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \to \mathcal{O}_X(D(f))\). This is exactly where the two
2274	  hypotheses enter. The bridge
2275	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} supplies, for a quasi-coherent
2276	  finite-type \(\mathcal{F}\), that \(\mathcal{O}_X(D(f)) = (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\)
2277	  and that the restriction \(\mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{F}(D(f))\) exhibits
2278	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(f))\) as the localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
2279	  \mathcal{F}(U)\); the algebra engine
2280	  \cref{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map} then yields, using that
2281	  \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is finitely generated, the required identity
2282	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{(\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f}\bigl((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
2283	  \mathcal{F}(U)\bigr) = \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U)) \cdot
2284	  (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\). Thus the local annihilators are compatible with basic-open
2285	  restriction, which is the patching datum that makes \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\)
2286	  a well-defined ideal sheaf. The finite-type hypothesis is genuinely required:
2287	  without finite generation of \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) the annihilator need not commute
2288	  with localization, and the local data fail to patch.
2289	
2290	  \emph{Note.} Mathlib does not carry an annihilator for sheaves of modules on a scheme.
2291	\end{definition}
2292	
2293	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2294	[Annihilator ideal bounded by the module annihilator on affine opens]
2295	  \label{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}
2296	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator_ideal_le}
2297	  \uses{def:modules_annihilator}
2298	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\mathcal{F}\) a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules and
2299	  \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open. Then the section over \(U\) of the annihilator
2300	  ideal sheaf \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}) is
2301	  contained in the module-annihilator of the sections of \(\mathcal{F}\) over
2302	  \(U\):
2303	  \[
2304	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
2305	    \;\subseteq\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr).
2306	  \]
2307	  This is the inclusion direction available directly from the definition of
2308	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\); the reverse inclusion is the basic-open coherence
2309	  statement gated on \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}. Proved directly
2310	  in Lean. It supports the well-definedness of the schematic support
2311	  (\cref{def:schematic_support}).
2312	\end{lemma}
2313	
2314	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2315	[Annihilator of a finitely generated module commutes with
2316	  localization]
2317	  \label{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map}
2318	  \lean{Module.annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map}
2319	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(S \subseteq R\) be a multiplicative
2320	  submonoid, and let \(R_S = S^{-1}R\) be the localization of \(R\) at \(S\). Let
2321	  \(M\) be a \emph{finitely generated} \(R\)-module and let \(f : M \to M_S\) be
2322	  an \(R\)-linear map exhibiting \(M_S\) as the localized module \(S^{-1}M\)
2323	  (i.e.\ \(f\) is a localization of \(M\) at \(S\)). Then the annihilator of
2324	  \(M_S\) over \(R_S\) is the extension of the annihilator of \(M\) along the
2325	  structure map \(R \to R_S\):
2326	  \[
2327	    \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)
2328	    \;=\; (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S
2329	    \;=\; \mathrm{map}\bigl(R \to R_S\bigr)\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}_R M\bigr).
2330	  \]
2331	  Equivalently, for finitely generated \(M\) the annihilator commutes with
2332	  localization: \(\mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M) = S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M)\).
2333	
2334	  \emph{The finite-generation hypothesis is essential.} For a module that is not
2335	  finitely generated the inclusion \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M) \subseteq
2336	  \mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M)\) can be strict, so the identity fails. This is the
2337	  standard commutative-algebra fact that the annihilator of a finite module
2338	  localizes; it is recorded here as the load-bearing algebra engine for the
2339	  basic-open coherence of \cref{def:modules_annihilator}.
2340	\end{lemma}
2341	
2342	\begin{proof}
2343	  \(\supseteq\): if \(r \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\), then \(r\) kills every element of
2344	  \(M\), hence its image \(r/1 \in R_S\) kills every element of \(M_S\) (each such
2345	  element is \(m/s = (1/s)\cdot f(m)\) for some \(m \in M\), \(s \in S\), and
2346	  \((r/1)\cdot(m/s) = (1/s)\cdot f(r\,m) = 0\)). Thus
2347	  \((\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\).
2348	
2349	  \(\subseteq\): write \(M = \langle m_1, \dots, m_n\rangle\) using the
2350	  finite-generation hypothesis, and let \(y \in \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\). Write
2351	  \(y = a/s\) with \(a \in R\), \(s \in S\). For each generator \(m_i\) the element
2352	  \(y\cdot f(m_i) = (a/s)\cdot f(m_i) = 0\) in \(M_S\); by the definition of the
2353	  localized module this means there is \(u_i \in S\) with \(u_i\,a\,m_i = 0\) in
2354	  \(M\). Put \(u = \prod_{i=1}^n u_i \in S\). Then \(u\,a\) kills every generator
2355	  \(m_i\), hence kills all of \(M\), so \(u\,a \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\). Finally
2356	  \(y = a/s = (u\,a)/(u\,s)\) lies in \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}_R M =
2357	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\). Therefore \(\mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S) \subseteq
2358	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\), and combined with the reverse inclusion the two
2359	  ideals coincide. The use of a single common multiplier \(u = \prod_i u_i\) is
2360	  exactly the step that requires finitely many generators.
2361	\end{proof}
2362	
2363	\begin{lemma}[Sections on a basic open localize the affine ring]
2364	  \label{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
2365	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_basicOpen}
2366	  \mathlibok
2367	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
2368	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}).}
2369	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open, and
2370	  \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then the restriction map
2371	  \(\Gamma(X, U) \to \Gamma\bigl(X,\, D(f)\bigr)\) on the basic open
2372	  \(D(f) = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,f\) exhibits \(\Gamma(X, D(f))\) as the
2373	  localization of \(\Gamma(X, U)\) away from \(f\); that is, it is
2374	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalization.Away}\,f\), so
2375	  \(\Gamma(X, D(f)) = (\Gamma(X, U))_f\) as a \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-algebra.
2376	\end{lemma}
2377	
2378	\begin{lemma}[Quasi-coherent sections on a basic open are the localized module]
2379	  \label{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
2380	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_basicOpen}
2381	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
2382	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Schemes" (chapter tag 020J), \S 7
2383	  % "Quasi-coherent sheaves on affines", Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves},
2384	  % item (4) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L691-L702).
2385	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2386	  % "Let $R$ be a ring. Let $M$ be an $R$-module. Let $\widetilde M$
2387	  %  be the sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
2388	  %  associated to $M$.
2389	  %  [...]
2390	  %  \item For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
2391	  %  as an $R_f$-module."
2392	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Schemes", \S 7, Lemma on the sheaves
2393	  \(\widetilde M\) on \(\Spec(R)\), item (4).}
2394	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a \emph{quasi-coherent} sheaf
2395	  of modules on \(X\). Let \(U \subseteq X\) be an
2396	  affine open and \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then:
2397	  \begin{enumerate}
2398	    \item \(\Gamma\bigl(X, D(f)\bigr)\) is the localization \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)
2399	      of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\)
2400	      (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); and
2401	    \item the section-restriction map
2402	      \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}\bigl(D(f)\bigr)\) exhibits
2403	      \(\mathcal{M}(D(f))\) as the localized module
2404	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\mathcal{M}(U)\); that is, it is a localization
2405	      of the \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(\mathcal{M}(U)\) at the submonoid
2406	      \(\mathrm{powers}\,f = \{1, f, f^2, \dots\}\).
2407	  \end{enumerate}
2408	\end{lemma}
2409	
2410	\begin{proof}
2411	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
2412	  Part (1) is \cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}, the Mathlib statement
2413	  that on an affine open \(U\) the sections over the basic open \(D(f)\) form the
2414	  localization of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\).
2415	
2416	  For part (2), identify the affine open \(U\) with \(\Spec \Gamma(X, U)\). Over
2417	  this affine, a quasi-coherent sheaf is \(\widetilde{N}\) for the
2418	  \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\), the sheaf associated to
2419	  \(N\) (no finiteness of \(N\) is required for this step). The Spec-local
2420	  computation of the sections of \(\widetilde{N}\) on a
2421	  basic open gives \(\Gamma\bigl(D(f), \widetilde{N}\bigr) = N_f\) as an
2422	  \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)-module, and the restriction map
2423	  \(\Gamma(\Spec \Gamma(X,U), \widetilde{N}) = N \to N_f\) is precisely the
2424	  localization homomorphism \(N \to (\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1} N\). Transporting
2425	  this identification across the affine isomorphism
2426	  \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, U)\) -- under which \(\mathcal{M}|_U\) corresponds to
2427	  \(\widetilde{N}\) by quasi-coherence and the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq U\)
2428	  corresponds to the standard open \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec\Gamma(X,U)\) -- shows
2429	  that the restriction \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}(D(f))\) is the
2430	  localization of \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\), i.e.\ it is a
2431	  localized module at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\). The substantive content is this
2432	  transport of the Spec-local localization fact for the sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\)
2433	  to a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{M}\) via the affine identification.
2434	\end{proof}

2454	  \emph{schematic support} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the closed subscheme of \(X\)

2465	\begin{definition}\leanok

2469	  \uses{def:schematic_support, def:modules_annihilator}
2470	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\). The
2471	  \emph{schematic-support immersion} is the canonical closed immersion
2472	  \[

2474	  \]

2483	\begin{lemma}[Properness as a morphism property]
2484	  \label{lem:isProper_mathlib}
2485	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper}
2486	  \mathlibok
2487	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
2488	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Proper}).}
2489	  A morphism \(f : X \to S\) of schemes is \emph{proper} iff it is separated,
2490	  universally closed, and locally of finite type. Properness is stable under base
2491	  change: if \(f : X \to S\) is proper and \(g : T \to S\) is any morphism then
2492	  the base change \(X \times_S T \to T\) is proper. Consequently the base-change
2493	  clause that Nitsure invokes (``properness \ldots\ preserved by base-change'')
2494	  applies directly to the proper-support condition below.
2495	\end{lemma}

2504	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors $\Hilb_{X/S}$ and
2505	  % $\Quot_{E/X/S}$" (read from

2507	  % + L418-L419).

2510	  %  such that the schematic support of $\F$ is proper over $T$

2518	  over \(S\) (along \(f\))} when the composite morphism

2554	\section{The Quot functor}
2555	\label{sec:quot_functor}
2556	
2557	\begin{definition}\leanok
2558	[Quot functor]
2559	  \label{def:quot_functor}
2560	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotFunctor}
2561	  \uses{def:hilbert_polynomial, def:has_proper_support}
2562	
2563	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) and
2564	  % \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\)" (read from
2565	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L394-L433
2566	  % + L481-L498).
2567	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2568	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(X\to S\) be a finite type
2569	  %  scheme over it. Let \(E\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X\).
2570	  %  Let \(Sch_S\) denote the category of all locally noetherian schemes
2571	  %  over \(S\). For any \(T\to S\) in \(Sch_S\), a \textbf{family of quotients of \(E\)
2572	  %  parametrised by \(T\)} will mean
2573	  %  a pair \((\F,q)\) consisting of
2574	  %
2575	  %  (i) a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on \(X_T = X\times_ST\)
2576	  %  such that the schematic support of \(\F\) is proper over \(T\)
2577	  %  and \(\F\) is flat over \(T\), together with
2578	  %
2579	  %  (ii) a surjective \({\mathcal O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism of sheaves
2580	  %  \(q:E_T  \to \F\) where \(E_T\) is the pull-back of \(E\) under
2581	  %  the projection \(X_T \to X\).
2582	  %
2583	  %  Two such families \((\F,q)\) and \((\F,q)\) parametrised by \(T\) will
2584	  %  be regarded as equivalent if \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\)),
2585	  %  and \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) will denote
2586	  %  an equivalence class. Then as properness and flatness are preserved
2587	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,
2588	  %  the pull-back of \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) under an \(S\)-morphism \(T'\to T\) is
2589	  %  well-defined, which gives
2590	  %  a set-valued contravariant functor
2591	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S} : Sch_S \to Sets\) under which
2592	  %  \(\)T\mapsto  \{\mbox{ All } \langle \F, q \rangle \mbox{ parametrised by }T\}\(\)
2593	  %  [...]
2594	  %  This shows that the functor \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\) naturally decomposes
2595	  %  as a co-product
2596	  %  \(\)\Quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\(\)
2597	  %  where for any polynomial \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\), the functor
2598	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) associates
2599	  %  to any \(T\) the set of all equivalence classes of
2600	  %  families \(\langle \F,q \rangle\) such that at each \(t\in T\) the
2601	  %  Hilbert polynomial of the restriction \(\F_t\), calculated
2602	  %  using the pull-back of \(L\), is \({\Phi}\)."
2603	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
2604	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
2605	  \(L\) a line bundle on \(X\), \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\), and
2606	  \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). The \emph{Quot functor with Hilbert
2607	  polynomial \(\Phi\)}
2608	  \[
2609	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set}
2610	  \]
2611	  sends an \(S\)-scheme \(T \to S\) to the set of equivalence classes
2612	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) of pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) where
2613	  \begin{enumerate}
2614	    \item \(\mathcal{F}\) is a coherent sheaf on \(X_T = X \times_S T\) whose
2615	      schematic support is proper over \(T\) (\cref{def:has_proper_support}) and
2616	      which is flat over \(T\);
2617	    \item \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) is a surjective
2618	      \(\mathcal{O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism, where \(E_T\) is the pull-back
2619	      of \(E\) along \(X_T \to X\);
2620	    \item for every \(t \in T\) the Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}\)
2621	      with respect to \(L|_{X_t}\) (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}) equals
2622	      \(\Phi\).
2623	  \end{enumerate}
2624	  Two pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) and \((\mathcal{F}', q')\) are equivalent iff
2625	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). On morphisms \(f : T' \to T\) over \(S\), the functor
2626	  acts by pulling back the quotient along \(X_{T'} \to X_T\) (well-defined
2627	  since tensor product is right-exact and preserves both flatness and
2628	  properness). The Hilbert scheme is the special case
2629	  \(\Hilb^{\Phi,L}_{X/S} := \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\).
2630	\end{definition}

2632	\section{The Grassmannian scheme}
2633	\label{sec:grassmannian_scheme}
2634	
2635	The Quot construction proceeds by embedding \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) as a
2636	locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian over \(S\). Since Mathlib carries
2637	no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} (only a finite-rank-quotient variety in
2638	linear algebra), we package the construction here.

2677	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(V\) be a locally free
2678	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\). For an integer \(1 \le d \le r\), the

2682	    \qquad

2685	    \text{ locally free of rank } d \}

2763	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(V\) a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module
2764	  of rank \(r\), and \(1 \le d \le r\). The functor \(\mathrm{Grass}(V,d)\) of
2765	  \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} is representable by a smooth projective
2766	  \(S\)-scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \to S\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\),
2767	  equipped with a tautological quotient
2768	  \(\pi^* V \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). The determinant
2769	  line bundle \(\det(\mathcal{U})\) is relatively very ample, and the
2770	  associated linear system gives a closed embedding
2771	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \hookrightarrow
2772	  \mathbb{P}_S\!\left(\bigwedge^d V\right)\) -- the \emph{Pl\"ucker embedding}.

2776	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the "Construction of Grassmannian" sub-section of
2777	% [Nitsure], \S 1 (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
2778	% L773-L930), which gives the construction in five named steps
2779	% ("Construction by gluing together affine patches", "Separatedness",
2780	% "Properness", "Universal quotient", "Projective embedding"). The total
2781	% verbatim text is ~150 lines; it is reproduced piecewise in the
2782	% SOURCE QUOTE comment of the theorem statement above.
2783	\begin{proof}
2784	  
2785	  Over \(S = \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) and \(V = \mathcal{O}_S^{\oplus r}\) the
2786	  Grassmannian \(\mathrm{Gr}(r, d)\) is constructed by gluing
2787	  \(\binom{r}{d}\) affine charts \(U^I = \Spec\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) indexed by
2788	  size-\(d\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\). The chart \(U^I\)
2789	  carries the matrix \(X^I\) of size \(d \times r\) whose \(I\)-th \(d \times d\)
2790	  minor is the identity, with the remaining \(d(r-d)\) entries free
2791	  variables (so \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\)). The
2792	  transition maps \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I \cap U^J \to U^J \cap U^I\) defined
2793	  by \(X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) on the locus where \(\det X^I_J\) is
2794	  invertible satisfy the cocycle condition
2795	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\). The glued
2796	  \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec\mathbb{Z}\) is smooth of relative dimension
2797	  \(d(r-d)\). Separatedness is checked from the diagonal
2798	  \(\Delta_{I,J} \subset U^I \times U^J\) cut out by \(X^J_I X^I - X^J = 0\).
2799	  Properness follows from the valuative criterion for DVRs: given
2800	  \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over a DVR \(R\) with fraction
2801	  field \(K\), factoring through some chart \(U^I\) via a ring map
2802	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\), choose \(J\) minimising the valuation
2803	  \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) of the minor; on the chart \(U^J\) all entries have
2804	  non-negative valuation, so \(f\) extends uniquely to \(\Spec R\).
2805	
2806	  The universal quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\oplus r}
2807	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) is given on \(U^I\) by the matrix \(X^I\)
2808	  and glued by \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I \cap U^J)\). The
2809	  determinant line bundle \(\det\mathcal{U}\) has transition functions
2810	  \(\det g_{I,J} = 1/P^I_J\); the global sections
2811	  \(\sigma_I \in \Gamma(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), \det\mathcal{U})\) defined by
2812	  \(\sigma_I|_{U^J} = P^J_I\) form a base-point-free linear system separating
2813	  points over \(\Spec\mathbb{Z}\), giving an embedding into
2814	  \(\mathbb{P}^{\binom{r}{d}-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\), which is closed by
2815	  properness. Representability of \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d)\) is then verified
2816	  on the universal pair \((\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), u)\): a quotient
2817	  \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\oplus r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of
2818	  rank \(d\) on \(T\) is everywhere locally trivialised; on the locus where
2819	  the columns indexed by \(I\) are a basis the matrix of \(q\) has the form
2820	  \(X^I\), defining the classifying morphism \(T \to U^I \subset
2821	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Base change from \(\mathbb{Z}\) to a general noetherian
2822	  base \(S\) with a vector bundle \(V\) is direct: trivialise \(V\) on an open
2823	  cover and glue. For non-trivial \(V\) the same construction produces
2824	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) with Pl\"ucker embedding into
2825	  \(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\).
2826	\end{proof}

2828	\section{Out of scope}
2829	\label{sec:quot_out_of_scope}
2830	
2831	This chapter packages \emph{only} the Quot-foundations layer: the Hilbert
2832	polynomial, the Quot functor, and the Grassmannian scheme with its
2833	representability. The following are explicitly out of scope here:
2834	\begin{itemize}
2835	  \item \textbf{Representability of the Quot scheme} -- the
2836	    Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman existence theorem and its proof skeleton
2837	    (boundedness, Grassmannian embedding, flattening stratification,
2838	    valuative criterion) are not constructed in this chapter.
2839	  \item \textbf{Flat base change of cohomology} (Stacks 02KH) and its
2840	    associated prerequisites -- not constructed in this chapter.
2841	  \item \textbf{Hilbert scheme as a separate chapter}. The Hilbert scheme
2842	    \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) is the special case
2843	    \(\Hilb_{X/S} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\) of the Quot functor and is
2844	    introduced inline (\cref{def:quot_functor}); no separate chapter.
2845	  \item \textbf{Snapper's Lemma} -- the polynomial-eventually property of
2846	    Euler characteristics, used in \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Cited
2847	    via Nitsure \S 1 and (textbook reference) Hartshorne III.5.2;
2848	    Lean formalization is a prerequisite sub-task.
2849	  \item \textbf{Identification with \(\mathbb{P}^n\) and relative
2850	    Grassmannian variants} (Nitsure \S 1, Exercises (1)--(4)) --
2851	    optional applications, not consumed by Route A.
2852	  \item \textbf{Group-scheme structure on \(\Pic_{C/k}\)} (A.2.c) -- the
2853	    abelian-group-scheme refinement is downstream. Its blueprint home is the
2854	    FGA Picard-representability assembly (the chapter that bundles the relative
2855	    Picard functor, its representability, and the abelian-group-scheme
2856	    structure on \(\Pic_{C/k}\)); that assembly lives in the parent project
2857	    which consumes this Quot-foundations layer and is not part of the present
2858	    subproject.
2859	  \item \textbf{Identity-component \(\Pic^0\) and abelian-variety
2860	    structure} (A.3) -- a separate Route A sub-row, fed by but not
2861	    constructed in this chapter.
2862	\end{itemize}

1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the classical Grothendieck/Nitsure~\S4
6	% \emph{generic flatness} theorem: over a noetherian integral base, a coherent
7	% sheaf on a finite-type \(X \to S\) becomes flat over a non-empty open
8	% subscheme of \(S\). Both its algebraic form (generic freeness of a finite
9	% module over a finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain) and its
10	% geometric form are recorded here as the prover-ready specification for
11	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}.

14	% Route~A.1.a relative-Spec chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}.
15	
16	\section{Setup and motivation}
17	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
18	
19	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
20	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
21	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
22	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
23	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
24	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
25	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
26	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
27	single non-zero element of the base ring.
28	
29	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
30	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
31	\(\F\).
32	
33	\section{Generic flatness}
34	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
35	
36	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
37	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
38	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
39	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
40	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
41	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
42	non-zero element of the base ring.
43	
44	\begin{theorem}
45	
46	
47	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
48	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
49	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatnessAlgebraic}
50	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_base, lem:noeth_prime_filtration, lem:gf_splice_shortExact,
51	        lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators, lem:gf_polynomial_core, lem:gf_finite_module}
52	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
53	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
54	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
55	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
56	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
57	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
58	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
59	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
60	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
61	  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
62	  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.
63	
64	\end{theorem}
65	
66	% INTENDED LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold pass creates the decl pinned above with
67	% exactly this signature):
68	%   (A B M : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
69	%     [CommRing B] [Algebra A B] [Algebra.FiniteType A B]

72	%     ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧
73	%       Module.Free (Localization.Away f) (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M)
74	
75	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Over the quotient field \(K\) of \(A\), the \(K\)-algebra
76	% \(B_K = K\otimes _AB\) is of finite type, and \(M_K = K\otimes_AM\) is a
77	% finite module over \(B_K\). Let \(n\) be the dimension of the support
78	% of \(M_K\) over \(\spec B_K\). We argue by induction on \(n\), starting
79	% with \(n=-1\) which is the case when \(M_K =0\). [...]
80	% Now let \(n\ge 0\), and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
81	% As \(B\) is noetherian and \(M\) is assumed to be a finite \(B\)-module,
82	% there exists a finite filtration
83	% \(0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M\)
84	% where each \(M_i\) is a \(B\)-submodule of \(M\) such that for each \(i\ge 1\)
85	% the quotient module \(M_i/M_{i-1}\) is isomorphic to \(B/\pp_i\) for
86	% some prime ideal \(\pp_i\) in \(B\). [...] By Noether normalisation lemma,
87	% there exist elements \(b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B\), such that \(K\otimes_AB\) is
88	% finite over its subalgebra \(K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]\) [...]"
89	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1772; full
90	% proof omitted for length, structural steps restated below in project notation.)
91	\begin{proof}
92	
93	  \textbf{Primary route — the finite \(A\)-module case.}
94	  Suppose first that \(M\) is module-finite over \(A\) itself. Since \(A\) is noetherian,
95	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). The generic fibre \(M_K = K \otimes_A M\)
96	  (where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\)) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free
97	  over \(K\); by \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} this freeness descends to a single basic open:
98	  there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\). This closes the
99	  theorem whenever \(M\) is finite over \(A\).
100

1951	  \((g' \circ f)_* = (f' \circ g)_*\) of the square's composite. (The
1952	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{g',f})^{\mathrm{hom}}\) collapse is a transparent re-association
1953	  that is most naturally accounted for together with the leg-reindex of step~(iii) rather than at
1954	  this point; the \(\Gamma\)-collapse identity itself is the same.) The congruence factor collapses
1955	  to a canonical transport along the equality of opens: its section-level value
1956	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardCongr}\,w)^{\mathrm{hom}}\) is the
1957	  presheaf-restriction along the canonical equality of opens, i.e.\ a repackaging along
1958	  that equality carrying no substantive content. Thus after the \(\Gamma\)-collapse the only
1959	  surviving non-trivial factor is \(f_{*}\bigl(\eta^{g'}_{\widetilde M}\bigr)\) read on global
1960	  sections.
1961	
1962	  \emph{(iii) Reduction to Seam~1 via the leg-reindex engine.} With the legs substituted
1963	  (step~(i)) to \(g' = e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A\), the leg-reindex engine
1964	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}, instantiated at \(a = e\),
1965	  \(b = \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A\), \(N = \widetilde M\), rewrites the unit factor
1966	  \(\eta^{g'} = \eta^{e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A}\), together with the
1967	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{e,\,\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A})\) coherence, into the affine
1968	  \((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)\)-unit followed by \((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)_{*}\) of the
1969	  \(e\)-unit. Since \(e\) is an isomorphism of schemes, its pullback functor is an equivalence
1970	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}) and the \(e\)-unit is invertible, hence an
1971	  isomorphism absorbed into the codomain identification \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\). The surviving
1972	  \((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)\)-unit has section value over \(\operatorname{Spec} A\) equal to
1973	  the algebraic unit \(\eta_M : m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\) of Seam~1
1974	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}). Composing the surviving factor with the
1975	  codomain identification \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\)
1976	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}) and reading over \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) by
1977	  restriction of scalars along \(\psi\) identifies all four factors with the restriction of
1978	  scalars along \(\psi\) of the codomain-read transport of the unit value, namely

1983	  named lemma; together they close the reindex without ever rewriting the bare legs.

2048	\begin{proof}
2049	  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read,
2050	        lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read, lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv,
2051	        lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso, lem:gammaPushforwardNatIso,
2052	        lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib, lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}
2053	  This crux reads the \((g^* \dashv g_*)\)-transpose of the inner composite
2054	  \(\theta_{\mathrm{in}}\) over \(\operatorname{Spec} R'\): it is the live remaining obligation
2055	  of the affine section-level computation, and the section identity
2056	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_section_identity} follows from it directly. The transpose is
2057	  the extension of scalars along \(\psi\) of the \(\operatorname{Spec} R\)-section reading
2058	  \(\rho : m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\) of \(\theta_{\mathrm{in}}\), whose value is the
2059	  affine unit value of Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}.
2060	
2061	  \emph{Counit factorization.} The base-change map
2062	  (Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}) is the adjunction transpose of
2063	  \(\theta_{\mathrm{in}}\) under \((g^* \dashv g_*)\) with \(g = \operatorname{Spec}\psi\). By the
2064	  counit form of the hom-set adjunction (\(\operatorname{homEquiv}\)-counit identity for the
2065	  transpose), it factors as \(g^*(\theta_{\mathrm{in}})\) followed by the counit
2066	  \(\varepsilon_g\), with no opaque transpose remaining:
2067	  \(\Gamma(\theta) = \Gamma\bigl(g^*(\theta_{\mathrm{in}})\bigr) \circ \Gamma(\varepsilon_g)\).
2068	
2069	  \emph{The concrete counit coherence — why it is not a bare computation.} Conjugating
2070	  \(g^*(\theta_{\mathrm{in}})\) followed by \(\varepsilon_g\) by the domain identification
2071	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read}) and the codomain
2072	  identification \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read})
2073	  requires the pullback dictionary \(\Pi_{\psi}\) of
2074	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} — which reads \(g^* = (\operatorname{Spec}\psi)^*\) of a
2075	  tilde as extension of scalars along \(\psi\) — to interact with the geometric counit
2076	  \(\varepsilon_g\). This interaction is \emph{not} a definitional reduction: \(\Pi_{\psi}\) is
2077	  built abstractly as the uniqueness-of-left-adjoints comparison, evaluated from the
2078	  natural-isomorphism of right adjoints \(\gamma_{\psi}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardNatIso})
2079	  via the conjugate-of-isomorphisms equivalence; concretely \(\Pi_{\psi}\) is the component at the
2080	  module of
2081	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{conjIso}(\mathrm{adj}_L, \mathrm{adj}_R)^{-1}(\gamma_{\psi})\bigr)^{-1}\),
2082	  where \(\mathrm{adj}_L = (\widetilde{(-)}_R \dashv \Gamma_R) . (g^* \dashv g_*)\) is the
2083	  composite ``tilde then pullback'' adjunction and
2084	  \(\mathrm{adj}_R = (\,{}^{\psi}\!(-) \dashv {}_{\psi}(-)) . (\widetilde{(-)}_{R'} \dashv
2085	  \Gamma_{R'})\) is the composite ``extension of scalars then tilde'' adjunction. Because the
2086	  dictionary is opaque in this way, the counit cannot be simplified against it by reflexivity
2087	  or by definitional unfolding; the identification must be made through the abstract conjugate calculus.
2088	
2089	  \emph{Conjugation via the composed-adjunction counit-triangle identity.} The required identity
2090	  is the counit-triangle (zig-zag) coherence for the \emph{composed} adjunction
2091	  \((\widetilde{(-)}_{R'} \dashv \Gamma_{R'}) . (g^* \dashv g_*)\), the same composite adjunction
2092	  whose left adjoint \(\Pi_{\psi}\) compares. Read concretely, this coherence states that the
2093	  dictionary \(\Pi_{\psi}\), composed with the geometric counit \(\varepsilon_g\) and the
2094	  tilde--\(\Gamma\) counit, equals the algebraic counit of the
2095	  extension/restriction-of-scalars adjunction \(({}^{\psi}\!(-) \dashv {}_{\psi}(-))\) along
2096	  \(\psi\); equivalently, conjugating \(\Gamma(\varepsilon_g)\) by the tilde dictionaries
2097	  identifies it with the module-level adjunction counit of
2098	  the extension/restriction-of-scalars adjunction along \(\psi\). This is the dual companion of the
2099	  unit-across-conjugate coherence (Lemma~\ref{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib}) used in Seam~1.
2100	  Applying it, conjugation replaces the transpose by extension of scalars along \(\psi\)
2101	  applied to the \(\operatorname{Spec} R\)-section reading \(\rho\) of \(\theta_{\mathrm{in}}\) —
2102	  the canonical \(R\)-linear map \(\rho : m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\), the affine unit
2103	  value of Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}: the geometric counit collapses to
2104	  the module-level counit, and the residual \(g^*\)-leg becomes \({}^{\psi}\!(-) = \) extension of
2105	  scalars along \(\psi\) applied to \(\rho\).
2106	
2107	  \emph{Identification on generators.} Since \(\rho : m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\), its
2108	  extension of scalars along \(\psi\) sends
2109	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto r' \cdot ((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m) = (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\). This
2110	  is precisely the inverse of the regrouping isomorphism
2111	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}) on generators, and both maps are
2112	  \(R'\)-linear, so they coincide.
2113	\end{proof}

2436	  \medskip
2437	  \emph{First reduction (locality on \(S'\)).} By
2438	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} it suffices to prove that the
2439	  base-change map of Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an
2440	  isomorphism on sections over every affine open of \(S'\). This is exactly the
2441	  locality reduction Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_map_affine_local}: restricting the
2442	  cartesian square to an affine open \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(R') \subseteq S'\) and
2443	  a chosen affine \(\operatorname{Spec}(R) \subseteq S\) containing \(g(U)\), the
2444	  hypothesis \([\mathrm{IsAffineHom}\ f]\) makes \(X\) restrict to
2445	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{F}\) makes it restrict
2446	  to \(\widetilde{M}\) for an \(A\)-module \(M\). (It is here that quasi-coherence is
2447	  used: it is what licenses writing \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\).) Computing the
2448	  fibre product gives \(X' = \operatorname{Spec}(R' \otimes_R A)\) and
2449	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) the tilde of the \((R' \otimes_R A)\)-module
2450	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\). The matter is thus reduced to the affine--affine
2451	  model, where the base-change map is a morphism \(\alpha\) of quasi-coherent
2452	  \((\operatorname{Spec} R')\)-modules.
2453	
2454	  \medskip
2455	  \emph{Passage to global sections.} The functor
2456	  \(\widetilde{(-)} : \operatorname{ModuleCat}(R') \to
2457	  (\operatorname{Spec} R').\mathrm{Modules}\) is fully faithful with essential image
2458	  the quasi-coherent modules; equivalently the counit
2459	  \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{N})} \to \mathcal{N}\) is an isomorphism exactly when
2460	  \(\mathcal{N}\) is quasi-coherent. Source and target of \(\alpha\) are
2461	  quasi-coherent, so both counits are isomorphisms and, by their naturality,
2462	  \[
2463	    \mathrm{IsIso}\,\alpha
2464	    \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
2465	    \mathrm{IsIso}\bigl(\Gamma(\alpha)\bigr),
2466	  \]
2467	  where \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is a concrete \(R'\)-linear map in
2468	  \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\). The whole computation therefore stays at the level
2469	  of modules.

2473	  computation Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange}, the map
2474	  \(\Gamma(\alpha)\), with its domain \(\Gamma(g^*(f_*\widetilde{M}))\) identified with
2475	  \(R' \otimes_R M\) and its codomain \(\Gamma(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde{M})\) identified
2476	  with \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) through the two dictionaries
2477	  Lemmas~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} and~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso},
2478	  \emph{is} the canonical cancellation isomorphism
2479	  \[
2480	    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} :
2481	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M,
2482	    \qquad (r' \otimes a) \otimes m \;\mapsto\; r' \otimes (a \cdot m)
2483	  \]
2484	  of \(R'\)-modules (Lemma~\ref{lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib}). This is the entire

2498	input that flatness preserves the finite equalizer computing \(H^0\). That input is

2501	\begin{lemma}[Flat base change preserves finite equalizers]
2502	  \label{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
2503	  \lean{LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv}
2504	  \mathlibok
2505	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
2506	  Let \(A \to B\) be a flat ring homomorphism (more generally, let \(B\) be a flat
2507	  \(A\)-module that is also an \(A\)-algebra, with the scalar-tower compatibility),
2508	  and let \(f, g : N \rightrightarrows P\) be a pair of \(A\)-linear maps. Write
2509	  \(\operatorname{eq}(f, g) = \{x \in N : f(x) = g(x)\}\) for their equalizer. Then the
2510	  canonical \(B\)-linear comparison map
2511	  \[
2512	    B \otimes_A \operatorname{eq}(f, g)
2513	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
2514	    \operatorname{eq}\bigl(\mathbf{1}_B \otimes f,\ \mathbf{1}_B \otimes g\bigr)
2515	  \]
2516	  is an isomorphism; that is, base change along a flat algebra commutes with the
2517	  formation of the finite equalizer (equivalently, of the kernel: the case
2518	  \(g = 0\) is \texttt{LinearMap.tensorKerEquiv}, underlain by
2519	  \texttt{Module.Flat.ker\_lTensor\_eq} and \texttt{Module.Flat.eqLocus\_lTensor\_eq}).
2520	  Since \(- \otimes_A B\) also preserves finite products, it follows that
2521	  \(- \otimes_A B\) carries the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) equalizer
2522	  \(\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \rightrightarrows
2523	    \prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})\) of a finite cover to the corresponding
2524	  equalizer of the base-changed cover.
2525	\end{lemma}

2557	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), the
2558	  \(i = 0\) case.}
2559	  Consider the cartesian square
2560	  \[
2561	    \begin{array}{ccc}
2562	      X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
2563	      {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]

2569	  is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. Then the base-change map of
2570	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism
2571	  \[

2573	    f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr) = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .

2583	  \(\mathcal{F}\).

2671	  \(S\); it is therefore the sheaf associated to the \(A\)-module
2672	  \(H^0(S, f_*\mathcal{F}) = H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\). Likewise
2673	  \(f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) is the sheaf on \(S'\) associated to the \(B\)-module
2674	  \(H^0(X', \mathcal{F}') = \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). Since pullback along \(g\)
2675	  corresponds to \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, the base-change map becomes the
2676	  comparison map of \(B\)-modules
2677	  \[
2678	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B),
2679	    \qquad X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)} \operatorname{Spec}(B),
2680	  \]
2681	  and it suffices to prove that this map is an isomorphism.
2682	
2683	  \medskip
2684	  \emph{\(H^0\) as a finite equalizer (the sheaf condition).} Choose a finite affine
2685	  open cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\) (possible since \(X\) is
2686	  quasi-compact). The sheaf condition for \(\mathcal{F}\) on this cover presents the
2687	  global sections as the equalizer of the two restriction maps
2688	  \[
2689	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})
2690	    \;=\;
2691	    \operatorname{eq}\Bigl(
2692	      \textstyle\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F})
2693	      \;\rightrightarrows\;
2694	      \textstyle\prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})
2695	    \Bigr),
2696	    \qquad U_{ij} = U_i \cap U_j,
2697	  \]
2698	  where the two parallel maps are \(s \mapsto (s_i|_{U_{ij}})\) and
2699	  \(s \mapsto (s_j|_{U_{ij}})\). This is precisely Čech degree \(0\) into degree
2700	  \(1\); it is the sheaf axiom and requires \emph{no} separatedness of \(X\) and no
2701	  higher cohomology.
2702	
2703	  \medskip
2704	  \emph{The separated case.} Suppose first that \(X\) is separated over \(A\). Then
2705	  every intersection \(U_{ij}\) is affine. Base changing the cover yields a finite
2706	  affine cover \(\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \dots \cup (U_t)_B\) of \(X_B\),
2707	  and the same sheaf-condition equalizer presents \(\Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). The
2708	  affine lemma Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward} (in its
2709	  \(i = 0\)/global-sections form) identifies each base-changed term with the original
2710	  term tensored over \(A\) with \(B\):
2711	  \[
2712	    \Gamma\bigl((U_i)_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
2713	    \qquad
2714	    \Gamma\bigl((U_{ij})_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
2715	  \]
2716	  compatibly with the two restriction maps. Hence the equalizer diagram for \(X_B\) is
2717	  the diagram for \(X\) with \(- \otimes_A B\) applied (using that \(- \otimes_A B\)
2718	  commutes with the finite products \(\prod_i\), \(\prod_{i,j}\)). Because \(A \to B\)
2719	  is flat, base change commutes with the formation of this finite equalizer
2720	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}):
2721	  \[
2722	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B
2723	    = \operatorname{eq}(\cdots) \otimes_A B
2724	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
2725	    \operatorname{eq}(\cdots \otimes_A B)
2726	    = \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B).
2727	  \]
2728	  This is exactly the comparison map of the reduction step, so it is an isomorphism.
2729	
2730	  \medskip
2731	  \emph{The quasi-separated case.} In general \(X\) need only be quasi-separated. We
2732	  argue by Mayer--Vietoris induction on the number \(t\) of members of a finite affine
2733	  cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\), each step reducing to the
2734	  separated/affine case. For \(t = 1\), \(X = U_1\) is affine and the claim is the
2735	  affine lemma. For \(t > 1\), set \(V = U_t\) (affine) and
2736	  \(W = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_{t-1}\) (an open covered by \(t - 1\) affines). Their
2737	  intersection \(W \cap V = \bigcup_{i < t}(U_i \cap U_t)\) is covered by the
2738	  \(t - 1\) opens \(U_i \cap U_t\), each of which is quasi-compact and separated
2739	  (an open of the affine, hence separated, \(U_i\)); thus \(W \cap V\) is separated
2740	  and quasi-compact, and flat base change holds for it by the separated case. The
2741	  two-member cover \(\{W, V\}\) of \(X\) gives the left-exact Mayer--Vietoris
2742	  sequence — itself the sheaf-condition equalizer for that cover —
2743	  \[
2744	    0 \longrightarrow \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})
2745	      \longrightarrow \Gamma(W, \mathcal{F}) \oplus \Gamma(V, \mathcal{F})
2746	      \longrightarrow \Gamma(W \cap V, \mathcal{F}),
2747	  \]
2748	  and likewise for \(X_B\) with \(\{W_B, V_B\}\). Flatness of \(A \to B\) makes
2749	  \(- \otimes_A B\) preserve this finite kernel/equalizer
2750	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}); since flat base change holds for
2751	  \(V\) (affine), for \(W\) (inductive hypothesis, \(t - 1\) members), and for
2752	  \(W \cap V\) (separated), it follows for \(X\). In degree \(0\) this gives
2753	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\), as required.
2754	\end{proof}

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its core properties --- universal property and affine
26	base --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
35	
36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}

1	\chapter{The Grassmannian over $\mathbb{Z}$: affine charts and gluing}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianCells}
3	
4	\section{Set-up: charts indexed by $d$-subsets}
5	\label{sec:gr_setup}
6	
7	Fix integers \(r \ge d \ge 1\). For a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M\) over a ring
8	and a subset \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\), write \(M_I\)
9	for the \emph{\(I\)-th minor} of \(M\): the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose
10	columns are those indexed by \(I\). We construct the Grassmannian scheme
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing one affine chart \(U^I\) for
12	each such \(I\). The construction is, conceptually, the quotient
13	\(\mathrm{GL}_{d,\mathbb{Z}} \backslash V\) of the scheme \(V\) of rank-\(d\)
14	matrices, but we never use the language of group-scheme actions: the charts and
15	their transition maps are elementary matrix algebra over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Taking
16	\(d = 1\) recovers \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,1) = \mathbb{P}^{r-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\).
17	
18	\begin{definition}
19	
20	\leanok
21	[Affine chart \(U^I\)]
22	  \label{def:gr_affine_chart}
23	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.affineChart}
24	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
25	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
26	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
27	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
28	  % "If $M$ is a $d\times r$-matrix, and
29	  %  $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with cardinality $\#(I)$ equal
30	  %  to $d$, the $I$ th minor $M_I$ of $M$ will
31	  %  mean the $d\times d$ minor of $M$ whose columns are indexed by
32	  %  $I$.
33	  %
34	  %  For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
35	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
36	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
37	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$.
38	  %  Let $\Z[X^I]$ denote the polynomial ring in the variables
39	  %  $x^I_{p,q}$, and let $U^I = \spec \Z[X^I]$, which is non-canonically
40	  %  isomorphic to the affine space $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$."
41	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
42	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). Let \(X^I\) denote the
43	  \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\)
44	  identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\) and whose remaining \(d(r - d)\) entries are
45	  independent indeterminates \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\),
46	  \(q \in \{1,\dots,r\} \setminus I\)) over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Write
47	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) for the polynomial ring in these \(d(r-d)\) variables and
48	  set
49	  \[
50	    U^I \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
51	  \]
52	  The choice of an ordering of the free entries gives a (non-canonical)
53	  isomorphism \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\); in particular
54	  \(U^I\) is a smooth affine \(\mathbb{Z}\)-scheme of relative dimension
55	  \(d(r-d)\). Intuitively a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-point of \(U^I\) is a \(d \times r\)
56	  matrix whose \(I\)-columns form the identity, i.e.\ a rank-\(d\) quotient of
57	  \(\mathbb{Z}^{r}\) trivialised on the coordinates \(I\).
58	\end{definition}
59	
60	\section{Transition maps and the cocycle condition}
61	\label{sec:gr_transition}
62	
63	\subsection*{Mathlib inputs for the transition map}
64	
65	The construction of \(\theta_{I,J}\) is a Cramer-inverse computation over the
66	localised chart ring, followed by one application of the universal property of a
67	localisation. We record the four Mathlib facts it rests on as dependency
68	anchors.
69	
70	\begin{lemma}
71	[Localised element is a unit]
72	  \label{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
73	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.algebraMap_isUnit}
74	  \mathlibok
75	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
76	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S\) a localisation of \(R\)
77	  away from \(x\) (i.e.\ \(S = R[1/x]\)). Then the image of \(x\) under the
78	  structure map \(R \to S\) is a unit of \(S\).
79	\end{lemma}
80

103	  \label{lem:mathlib_away_lift}

106	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
107	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S = R[1/x]\) a localisation
108	  of \(R\) away from \(x\). Given any ring homomorphism \(g : R \to P\) that
109	  sends \(x\) to a unit of \(P\), there is a unique ring homomorphism
110	  \(S \to P\) extending \(g\) along \(R \to S\).

113	\subsection*{The transition map by matrix algebra}

362	[The pre-hom sends \(P^J_I\) to a unit]
363	  \label{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit}
364	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_transitionPreMap_minorDet}
365	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
366	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
367	  % affine patches" (read from
368	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
369	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
370	  % "In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
371	  %  so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$."
372	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
373	  %   `isUnit_transitionPreMap_minorDet d r I J :`
374	  %   `IsUnit (transitionPreMap d r I J (minorDet d r J I))`.
375	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
376	  The pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre})
377	  sends \(P^J_I\) (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}, the \(I\)-minor determinant of
378	  \(X^J\)) to a unit of \(R^I_J\). Indeed
379	  \[
380	    \tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I\bigr)
381	      = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = \det(X^I_J)^{-1} = 1/P^I_J,
382	  \]
383	  which is a unit.
384	\end{lemma}
385	\begin{proof}
386	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
387	  \leanok
388	  By \cref{def:gr_transition_pre}, \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = M = (X^I_J)^{-1}
389	  X^I\), so \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) carries the \(I\)-minor \(X^J_I\) of \(X^J\) to
390	  the \(I\)-minor of \(M\), which is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) since
391	  the \(I\)-minor of \(X^I\) is the identity. Hence \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) =
392	  \det((X^I_J)^{-1}) = \det(X^I_J)^{-1}\). As \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit
393	  (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}), so is its inverse; the matrix-inverse
394	  determinant identity uses \cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}.
395	\end{proof}

424	  away-localisation (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) provides a unique ring
425	  homomorphism extending it along \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I]\).
426	  This extension is the \emph{transition map}
427	  \[
428	    \theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \longrightarrow
429	    \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J], \qquad \theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I .
430	  \]

438	\leanok
439	[\(\theta_{I,I}\) is the identity]
440	  \label{lem:gr_transition_self}
441	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionMap_self}
442	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_minor_det}
443	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
444	  % affine patches" (read from
445	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L838-L848).
446	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
447	  % "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$".

449	  %   `transitionMap_self d r I : transitionMap d r I I = RingHom.id _`
450	  %   (up to the identification `R^I_I = R^I` from `P^I_I = 1`).
451	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
452	  For every \(I\), \(\theta_{I,I} = \id_{U^I}\): since \(P^I_I = 1\)
453	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) the minor \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\) is its own
454	  inverse, so \(\theta_{I,I}(X^I) = (X^I_I)^{-1} X^I = X^I\), i.e.\
455	  \(\theta_{I,I}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) is the identity ring homomorphism
456	  of \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_I] = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\).
457	\end{lemma}
458	\begin{proof}
459	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_minor_det}
460	  \leanok
461	  When \(J = I\) one has \(P^I_I = \det(1_{d\times d}) = 1\)
462	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}), so \(R^I_I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\), the minor \(X^I_I\)
463	  is the identity, and its Cramer inverse is again the identity. Hence the image
464	  matrix is \(M = 1 \cdot X^I = X^I\), the pre-hom sends each \(x^I_{p,q}\) to
465	  itself, and its localisation lift (\cref{def:gr_transition}) is the identity.
466	\end{proof}

80	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
81	
82	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open \(U = \Spec R \subseteq X\) one
83	sets \(\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) regarded as an \(R\)-algebra, and the local pieces
84	glue compatibly because \(\mathcal{A}\) is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
85	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for an inclusion
86	\(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\) gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).

101	  %  \(\)
102	  %  with the following properties:
103	  %  \begin{enumerate}

105	  %  \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \to \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\), and
106	  %  \item for \(U \subset U' \subset S\) affine open the composition
107	  %  \(\)
108	  %  \xymatrix{

117	  %  Moreover, \(\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})\)

119	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec);
120	  cf.\ [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(a).}
121	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra
122	  (\cref{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}). There exists a scheme
123	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) together with an affine morphism
124	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) such that
125	  \begin{enumerate}
126	    \item for every affine open \(U \subseteq X\), there is an isomorphism
127	      \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \xrightarrow{\sim} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\);
128	    \item for \(U \subseteq U' \subseteq X\) both affine open, the composite
129	      $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \xrightarrow{i_U^{-1}} \pi^{-1}(U) \hookrightarrow
130	      \pi^{-1}(U') \xrightarrow{i_{U'}} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$
131	      coincides with the open immersion induced by the restriction
132	      \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\).
133	  \end{enumerate}
134	  The pair \((\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}), \pi)\) is unique up to unique isomorphism
135	  over \(X\).

138	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Follows immediately from
139	% Lemmas REF,
140	% REF, and
141	% REF.
142	% Uniqueness is stated in the last sentence of
143	% Lemma REF."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  
146	  
147	\leanok
148	  By the open-cover gluing principle for schemes (Mathlib
149	  \texttt{Scheme.GlueData} / \texttt{AffineScheme.glueOpens}) it suffices to specify
150	  affine local pieces \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) for each affine open \(U \subseteq X\) and
151	  open-immersion transition data
152	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \hookrightarrow \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\) for \(U \subseteq U'\)
153	  satisfying the cocycle condition. The transition morphisms come from the ring maps
154	  \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\); quasi-coherence yields the cartesian square
155	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) = U \times_{U'} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\), so each transition is an
156	  open immersion. Transitivity (Stacks lemma-transitive-spec) gives the cocycle condition.
157	  Uniqueness of the glued scheme is the standard property of the gluing functor.
158	\end{proof}

266	  % iter-180+). Consumers may now rely on the affineness conclusion, but the
267	  % Yoneda-bijection witness is still deferred.
268	  Following Stacks tag 01LQ (proof of lemma-spec): the functor \(F\) is a Zariski sheaf
269	  because morphisms of schemes and \(\mathcal{O}\)-algebra maps both glue under open covers.
270	  Choose an affine open cover \(X = \bigcup U_i\). The subfunctor
271	  \(F_i \subseteq F\) of pairs \((f, \varphi)\) with \(f(T) \subseteq U_i\) is representable by
272	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U_i))\) via the affine-base case
273	  (\cref{thm:relative_spec_affine_base}) together with the
274	  restriction identification \(F_i \cong F_{U_i,\,\mathcal{A}|_{U_i}}\).
275	  Each \(F_i \hookrightarrow F\) is an open
276	  subfunctor (pull back \(U_i\) along \(f\)). The \(F_i\) jointly cover \(F\) since the \(U_i\)
277	  cover \(X\). The gluing of representable open subfunctors produces a representing scheme,
278	  which is canonically isomorphic to the glued scheme \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\)
279	  of \cref{thm:relative_spec_exists}.
280	\end{proof}

293	  % ``affine_base_iff'' is also misleading (the encoded type is not an iff).

295	  % is available.

308	  \[
309	    \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \;\;\cong\;\; \Spec(A).
310	  \]

312	  spectrum of the global sections.

314	
315	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Write \(S = \Spec(R)\) and \(A = \Gamma(S, \mathcal{A})\).
316	% Then \(A\) is an \(R\)-algebra and \(\mathcal{A} = \widetilde A\).
317	% The ring map \(R \to A\) gives rise to a canonical map

319	% f_{univ} : \Spec(A)
320	% \longrightarrow
321	% S = \Spec(R).
322	% \(\)
323	% We have
324	% \(f_{univ}^*\mathcal{A} =  \widetilde{A \otimes_R A}\)
325	% by Schemes, Lemma REF.
326	% Hence there is a canonical map
327	% \(\)
328	% \varphi_{univ} :
329	% f_{univ}^*\mathcal{A} = \widetilde{A \otimes_R A}
330	% \longrightarrow
331	% \widetilde A = \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(A)}
332	% \(\)
333	% coming from the \(A\)-module map \(A \otimes_R A \to A\),
334	% \(a \otimes a' \mapsto aa'\). We claim that the pair
335	% \((f_{univ}, \varphi_{univ})\) represents \(F\) in this case."

338	  \leanok
339	  % NOTE (iter-179 review): supersedes the iter-176 placeholder-discharge note.
340	  % The iter-176 ``change IsAffine (Spec R); inferInstance`` form is now stale
341	  % — iter-179 Lane B closes via ``UniversalProperty 𝒜`` + ``isAffine_of_isAffineHom``

346	  Write \(X = \Spec R\) and \(A = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A})\). Quasi-coherence of
347	  \(\mathcal{A}\) gives \(\mathcal{A} = \widetilde{A}\) as \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
348	  (with the \(R\)-algebra structure on \(A\) inducing the algebra structure on
349	  \(\widetilde A\)). The ring map \(R \to A\) gives a morphism
350	  \(f_{\mathrm{univ}} : \Spec A \to \Spec R = X\) and a canonical
351	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A}\)-algebra map

353	  \to \mathcal{O}_{\Spec A} = \widetilde A$ given by multiplication
354	  \(a \otimes a' \mapsto aa'\). We verify \((\Spec A, f_{\mathrm{univ}}, \varphi_{\mathrm{univ}})\)
355	  represents \(F\) on the affine base: given a test scheme \(T\) and a pair
356	  \((f, \varphi)\), the composite

358	  \Gamma(T, f^*\mathcal{A}) \xrightarrow{\varphi} \Gamma(T, \mathcal{O}_T)$

362	  \texttt{Scheme.Hom.toSpec}). One checks this assignment inverts the universal map
363	  \((f, \varphi) \mapsto a\) defined by \(f = f_{\mathrm{univ}} \circ a\),
364	  \(\varphi = a^* \varphi_{\mathrm{univ}}\).
