367	\section{Lean encoding}
368	\label{sec:relspec_lean_encoding}
369	
370	The Lean target file is the new \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}.
371	The construction proceeds by gluing absolute affine pieces: for each affine open
372	\(U \subseteq X\) with \(U = \Spec R\) and \(A := \mathcal{A}(U)\) regarded as an
373	\(R\)-algebra, the local piece is \(\Spec A\) with structure morphism \(\Spec A \to \Spec R = U\)
374	induced by the algebra unit \(R \to A\). Mathlib provides \texttt{IsAffineOpen.toScheme} for
375	the affine-open inclusion and the absolute \texttt{Scheme.Spec} functor for the local
376	pieces; the transition isomorphisms come from quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{A}\) via
377	\texttt{QuasiCoherent.tensorIso} (or the explicit
378	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for \(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\)).
379	The gluing backbone is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData} (general construction) or
380	the convenience wrapper \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme.glueOpens}; the cocycle
381	condition reduces to Stacks \texttt{lemma-transitive-spec}. The universal property
382	(\cref{thm:relative_spec_univ}) is expressed as a
383	\texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} witness, providing the natural bijection
384	$\Hom_X(T, \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})) \cong
385	\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X\text{-alg}}(\mathcal{A}, g_*\mathcal{O}_T)$ as a
386	\texttt{Yoneda}-style structure. No new core axiom is required; the entire chapter is a
387	definitional/structural construction on top of existing Mathlib affine-scheme
388	infrastructure.
389	
390	\section{Out of scope}
391	\label{sec:relspec_out_of_scope}
392	
393	This chapter packages \emph{only} the relative-spectrum construction and its core
394	properties. The following downstream constructions live in separate sibling chapters and
395	are explicitly out of scope here:
396	\begin{itemize}
397	  \item \textbf{Relative Picard functor \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\)} (A.1.c) --- consumed-by, not
398	    defined-here; its blueprint home is the planned chapter
399	    \texttt{Picard\_RelPicFunctor.tex}.
400	  \item \textbf{Line-bundle pullback on a product \(C \times_k T\)} (A.1.b) ---
401	    \texttt{Picard\_LineBundlePullback.tex} (planned).
402	  \item \textbf{Connectedness, dimensionality, smoothness of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\)}
403	    --- downstream properties that depend on assumptions on \(\mathcal{A}\)
404	    (locally finite type, flat, etc.), not unconditional consequences of the construction.
405	  \item \textbf{Identity-component \(\Pic^0\) and abelian-variety structure} (A.3) --- a
406	    separate Route~A sub-row, fed by but not constructed in this chapter.

100	
101	  \textbf{Surviving residue — reducing the finite-type \(B\)-algebra case to the
102	  finite \(A\)-module case.} The genuine content not supplied by Mathlib is the
103	  reduction of the general hypothesis (\(M\) finite over a \emph{finite-type}
104	  \(A\)-algebra \(B\)) to the finite \(A\)-module situation handled above. Concretely,
105	  the missing piece is the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness: a finite module
106	  over \(A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) becomes free over \(A_f\) after inverting a single
107	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). The classical \S4 dévissage below is the fallback route
108	  for exactly this residue.
109	
110	  \emph{Fallback (Nitsure \S4 induction).}
111	  The \S4 dévissage is assembled from the sub-lemmas of
112	  \cref{sec:gf_devissage_chain}. If \(M_K = 0\), the torsion base case
113	  \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} gives \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f = 0\) free. Otherwise the
114	  prime-filtration induction principle \cref{lem:noeth_prime_filtration} over the
115	  noetherian ring \(B\), combined with the splicing fact
116	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}, reduces to \(M = B/\mathfrak{p}\) and hence to
117	  \(B\) a domain, \(M = B\); Noether normalisation with clearing of denominators
118	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators} makes \(B_g\) finite over a polynomial
119	  ring \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), and the polynomial-ring core
120	  \cref{lem:gf_polynomial_core} — bottoming out at the finite-\(A\)-module leaf
121	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} of the primary route — produces the required
122	  \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\).
123	\end{proof}
124	
125	\subsection{Dévissage chain for the finite-type residue}
126	\label{sec:gf_devissage_chain}
127	
128	The following sub-lemmas decompose the surviving residue of
129	\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} — the case where \(M\) is finite over the
130	finite-type \(A\)-algebra \(B\) but not module-finite over \(A\) — into the
131	classical \cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 induction. They are ordered so that each block
132	depends only on earlier blocks or Mathlib lemmas. Throughout,
133	\(A\) is a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), \(B\) a
134	finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module with the compatible
135	\(A\)-module structure via the scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\).
136	
137	\begin{lemma}[Finite-module leaf — finite \(A\)-module case]
138	  \label{lem:gf_finite_module}
139	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite}
140	  \uses{lem:fp_free_descent}
141	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there
142	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
143	\end{lemma}
144	
145	\begin{proof}
146	
147	  This is the \(d = 0\) leaf of the induction. A finite module over a noetherian ring is
148	  finitely presented, so
149	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). Over the generic point
150	  \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A) = S^{-1}A\) (\(S = A \setminus \{0\}\)) the localisation
151	  \(M_K = S^{-1}M\) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free over
152	  \(K\). The finite-presentation free-descent lemma \cref{lem:fp_free_descent}
153	  then descends this freeness to a single basic open: there is \(r \in S\), i.e.\
154	  \(r \in A\) with \(r \ne 0\), such that \(M_r\) is free over \(A_r\). Take
155	  \(f := r\).
156	\end{proof}
157	
158	\begin{lemma}[Finite-presentation free descent]
159	  \label{lem:fp_free_descent}
160	  \lean{Module.FinitePresentation.exists_free_localizedModule_powers}
161	  \mathlibok
162	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
163	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(S \subseteq R\) a multiplicative subset, and
164	  \(M\) a finitely-presented \(R\)-module such that the localisation \(M_S\) is
165	  free over the localisation \(R_S\). Then there exists \(r \in S\) such that
166	  \(M_r\) is free over \(R_r\) (of the same finite rank).
167	\end{lemma}
168	
169	\begin{lemma}[L1 — torsion base case \(M_K = 0\)]
170	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_base}
171	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_torsion}
172	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case
173	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1726).
174	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
175	  % "We argue by induction on $n$, starting with $n=-1$ which is the case when
176	  %  $M_K =0$. In this case, as $K\otimes _AM = S^{-1}M$ where $S = A - \{ 0\}$,
177	  %  each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
178	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating elements,
179	  %  we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$. Hence $M_f =0$, proving
180	  %  the lemma when $n=-1$."
181	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (base case of the
182	  induction).}
183	  Suppose \(K \otimes_A M = 0\), where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Then there exists
184	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(f M = 0\); consequently \(M_f = 0\) is free over
185	  \(A_f\).
186	\end{lemma}
187	
188	\begin{proof}
189	
190	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1719-L1726).
191	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (base case).}
192	  Since \(K \otimes_A M = S^{-1}M\) with \(S = A \setminus \{0\}\), the hypothesis
193	  \(M_K = 0\) means every element of \(M\) is killed by some non-zero element of
194	  \(A\). Choose a finite \(B\)-generating set \(v_1, \dots, v_k\) of \(M\) (which
195	  exists because \(M\) is a finite \(B\)-module), and for each \(i\) an
196	  \(a_i \in A\), \(a_i \ne 0\), with \(a_i v_i = 0\). The product
197	  \(f := a_1 \cdots a_k\) is non-zero (\(A\) is a domain) and annihilates every
198	  generator, hence \(f M = 0\). Therefore \(M_f = 0\), which is free over \(A_f\).
199	\end{proof}
200	
201	\begin{lemma}[L2 — prime-filtration induction principle]
202	  \label{lem:noeth_prime_filtration}
203	  \lean{IsNoetherianRing.induction_on_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime}
204	  \mathlibok
205	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
206	  Let \(B\) be a noetherian commutative ring and let \(P\) be a predicate on finite
207	  \(B\)-modules that (i) holds for every subsingleton module, (ii) holds for every
208	  cyclic quotient \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) with \(\mathfrak{p}\) prime, and (iii) is
209	  closed under short exact sequences (\(P(N_1)\) and \(P(N_3)\) imply \(P(N_2)\)
210	  for \(0 \to N_1 \to N_2 \to N_3 \to 0\)). Then \(P\) holds for every finite
211	  \(B\)-module. In particular every finite \(B\)-module \(M\) admits a finite
212	  filtration \(0 = M_0 \subset \cdots \subset M_r = M\) with each quotient
213	  \(M_i / M_{i-1} \cong B/\mathfrak{p}_i\) for a prime \(\mathfrak{p}_i\), so to
214	  prove generic freeness for \(M\) it suffices to prove it for each
215	  \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) and to splice along the filtration's short exact sequences.
216	\end{lemma}
217	
218	\begin{lemma}[L3a — localisation of a short exact sequence is exact]
219	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact}
220	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exact_localizedModule_powers_of_shortExact}
221	  Let \(0 \to M' \xrightarrow{i} M \xrightarrow{q} M'' \to 0\) be a short exact
222	  sequence of \(B\)-modules, and let \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). Restricting scalars
223	  along \(A \to B\) and localising every term at the powers of \(f\) yields a short
224	  exact sequence
225	  \[
226	    0 \to M'_f \xrightarrow{i_f} M_f \xrightarrow{q_f} M''_f \to 0
227	  \]
228	  of \(A_f\)-modules; in particular \(i_f\) is injective and \(q_f\) is surjective.
229	\end{lemma}
230	
231	\begin{proof}
232	
233	  Localisation at a multiplicative set is an exact functor, preserving injections,
234	  surjections, and exactness. Applying it to the scalar-restricted \(A\)-linear maps
235	  \(i\) and \(q\) (via \(A \to B\)) returns the displayed sequence. Proved directly in Lean.
236	\end{proof}
237	
238	\begin{lemma}[L3b — free transport across a finer localisation]
239	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport}
240	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_free_of_eq_mul}
241	  Let \(N\) be a \(B\)-module and \(f', f'' \in A\) with \(f = f' f''\). If the
242	  localisation \(N_{f'}\) is free over \(A_{f'}\), then the further localisation
243	  \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
244	\end{lemma}
245	
246	\begin{proof}
247	
248	  Because \(f = f' f''\), the localisation away from \(f\) factors through the
249	  localisation away from \(f'\): the ring \(A_f\) is the localisation of \(A_{f'}\)
250	  at the image of \(f''\), and likewise the module \(N_f\) is the localisation of
251	  \(N_{f'}\) at the image of \(f''\). A localisation of a free module is free over
252	  the localised base ring — a basis of \(N_{f'}\) over \(A_{f'}\) maps to a basis of
253	  \(N_f\) over \(A_f\). Hence \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Stated as a standalone
254	  fact, it is applied to both ends of the spliced sequence.
255	\end{proof}
256	
257	\begin{lemma}[L3c — a short exact sequence with free ends has a free middle]
258	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
259	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_of_shortExact_of_free_free}
260	  Let \(0 \to M'_f \to M_f \to M''_f \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of
261	  \(A_f\)-modules in which both ends \(M'_f\) and \(M''_f\) are free over \(A_f\).
262	  Then \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\); concretely \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\).
263	\end{lemma}
264	
265	\begin{proof}
266	
267	  A free module is projective, so the free quotient \(M''_f\) is projective and the
268	  short exact sequence splits: \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\). A finite direct sum
269	  of free \(A_f\)-modules is free, hence \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
270	\end{proof}
271	
272	\begin{lemma}[L3 — splicing fact for a short exact sequence (assembly)]
273	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}
274	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_shortExact}
275	  \uses{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact,
276	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
277	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, splicing remark
278	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1737-L1745).
279	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
280	  % "Note that if $0\to M'\to M\to M'' \to 0$ is a short exact sequence of
281	  %  $B$-modules, and if $f'$ and $f''$ are non-zero elements of $A$ such that
282	  %  $M'_{f'}$ and $M''_{f''}$ are free over respectively $A_{f'}$ and $A_{f''}$,
283	  %  then $M_f$ is a free module over $A_f$ where $f=f'f''$. We will use this fact
284	  %  repeatedly. Therefore it is enough to prove the result when $M$ is of the form
285	  %  $B/\pp$ for a prime ideal $\pp$ in $B$. This reduces us to the case where $B$
286	  %  is a domain and $M=B$."
287	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (splicing fact).}
288	  Let \(0 \to M' \to M \to M'' \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of \(B\)-modules,
289	  and let \(f', f'' \in A\) be non-zero with \(M'_{f'}\) free over \(A_{f'}\) and
290	  \(M''_{f''}\) free over \(A_{f''}\). Then for \(f := f' f''\) the localisation
291	  \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
292	\end{lemma}
293	
294	\begin{proof}
295	  \uses{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact,
296	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
297	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1737-L1745).
298	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (splicing fact).}
299	  Set \(f := f' f''\); since \(A\) is a domain and \(f', f'' \ne 0\), the product is
300	  non-zero, so \(f \ne 0\). Localising the given short exact sequence at the powers
301	  of \(f\) (after restricting scalars to \(A\)) produces a short exact sequence
302	  \(0 \to M'_f \to M_f \to M''_f \to 0\) of \(A_f\)-modules, by
303	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact}. Both ends are free over \(A_f\):
304	  applying \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport} to \(M'\) with the
305	  factorisation \(f = f' f''\) (using that \(M'_{f'}\) is free over \(A_{f'}\)) shows
306	  \(M'_f\) is free over \(A_f\), and applying the same lemma to \(M''\) with the
307	  factorisation \(f = f'' f'\) (using that \(M''_{f''}\) is free over \(A_{f''}\))
308	  shows \(M''_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Finally
309	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split} concludes that the middle term \(M_f\) is
310	  free over \(A_f\).
311	\end{proof}

313	\begin{lemma}[L4 anchor — Noether normalisation over a field]
314	  \label{lem:noether_normalization_fg}
315	  \lean{exists_finite_inj_algHom_of_fg}
316	  \mathlibok
317	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
318	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(R\) a non-trivial finite-type \(k\)-algebra. Then there
319	  exist \(s \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective \(k\)-algebra homomorphism
320	  \(k[X_1, \dots, X_s] \to R\) that is module-finite, i.e.\ \(R\) is a finite module

324	\begin{lemma}
325	
326	[L4a — clearing one equation's denominators]
327	  \label{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
328	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_clear_one_denominator}
329	  % NOTE (shared engine): the denominator-clearing performed here -- pushing finitely
330	  % many coefficients of one polynomial from K = Frac(A) down into a single
331	  % Localization.Away g -- is the same primitive the torsion reindex (L5b,
332	  % lem:gf_torsion_reindex) uses when it inverts the leading coefficient of the
333	  % Nagata-normalised annihilator. A prover who writes one general "clear finitely many
334	  % denominators into one Localization.Away g" helper can have both lemmas consume it.
335	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, normalisation step
336	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1755-L1759).
337	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
338	  % "If $g\ne 0$ in $A$ is chosen to be a `common denominator' for
339	  %  coefficients of equations of integral dependence
340	  %  satisfied by a finite set of
341	  %  algebra generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$,
342	  %  we see that $B_g$ is finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
343	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
344	  %   Let K := FractionRing A and A_g := Localization.Away g; the canonical map
345	  %   A_g -> K (induced because powers of g are non-zero-divisors of A) extends along
346	  %   MvPolynomial.map to MvPolynomial (Fin n) A_g -> MvPolynomial (Fin n) K.
347	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] (n : ℕ)
348	  %       (p : MvPolynomial (Fin n) (FractionRing A)) :
349	  %       ∃ (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0),
350	  %         p ∈ Set.range
351	  %           (MvPolynomial.map (algebraMap (Localization.Away g) (FractionRing A)))
352	  % NOTE (encoding): there is NO canonical `Algebra (Localization.Away g)
353	  %   (FractionRing A)` instance, so the landed Lean does not use the literal
354	  %   `algebraMap (Localization.Away g) (FractionRing A)` above. It encodes the same map
355	  %   as `IsLocalization.map (FractionRing A) (RingHom.id A) hle` with
356	  %   `hle : powers g ≤ comap id (nonZeroDivisors A)` — mathematically identical, just the
357	  %   well-typed form. The proof is axiom-clean.
358	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (common denominator).}
359	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), and let
360	  \(p \in K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) be a polynomial with coefficients in \(K\) (for instance,
361	  one coefficient polynomial of an equation of integral dependence over
362	  \(K[b_1, \dots, b_n]\)). Then there exists \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), such that \(p\)
363	  lies in the image of the coefficient-extension map
364	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\); equivalently, \(p\) has all of its
365	  coefficients in \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\).
366	\end{lemma}
367	
368	\begin{proof}
369	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1755-L1759).
370	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (common denominator).}
371	  The polynomial \(p\) has finitely many non-zero coefficients
372	  \(c_1, \dots, c_r \in K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Each \(c_j\) is a fraction
373	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) with \(a_j \in A\) and \(s_j \in A \setminus \{0\}\). Put
374	  \(g := s_1 \cdots s_r \in A\); since \(A\) is a domain and each \(s_j \ne 0\), also
375	  \(g \ne 0\). For each \(j\) the denominator \(s_j\) divides \(g\), so
376	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) lies in the subring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\). Hence every
377	  coefficient of \(p\) lies in \(A_g\), and \(p\) is the coefficient-extension of a
378	  polynomial in \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
379	\end{proof}

426	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (Noether
427	  normalisation with denominators).}
428	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K\) and \(B\) a finite-type
429	  \(A\)-algebra that is a domain with \(B_K = K \otimes_A B \ne 0\). Write
430	  \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\) and \(B_g = B[g^{-1}]\) for the localisations away from \(g\)
431	  and its image. Then there exist a natural number \(n\), an element \(g \in A\) with
432	  \(g \ne 0\), and an injective \(A_g\)-algebra homomorphism
433	  \[
434	    \varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \longrightarrow B_g,
435	  \]
436	  where \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is the polynomial ring on \(n\) algebraically
437	  independent variables, such that \(B_g\) is module-finite over the image polynomial
438	  subalgebra via \(\varphi\). The injectivity of \(\varphi\) records the algebraic
439	  independence of the generators \(b_j := \varphi(X_j)\), and module-finiteness is
440	  taken over the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\).
441	\end{lemma}
442	
443	\begin{proof}
444	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
445	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1747-L1758).
446	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (Noether normalisation with denominators).}
447	  % NOTE: the denominator-clearing middle of this proof is a
448	  % Finset-fold of lem:gf_clear_one_denominator over the finite generating set. Each
449	  % generator satisfies one equation of integral dependence whose coefficient
450	  % polynomials lie in K[b_1,...,b_n]; gf_clear_one_denominator clears the denominators
451	  % of one such coefficient polynomial into a single Localization.Away g_i. Folding over
452	  % the finite set of (generator, coefficient) pairs and multiplying the finitely many
453	  % g_i yields one common g != 0 over which all integrality relations hold; the AlgHom
454	  % assembly below then runs over A_g exactly as before.
455	
456	  \emph{Step 1 — Noether normalisation over \(K\).} Apply Noether normalisation over the
457	  field \(K\) (\cref{lem:noether_normalization_fg}) to the finite-type \(K\)-algebra
458	  \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\): there are algebraically independent
459	  \(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n \in B_K\) with \(B_K\) module-finite over
460	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). Clearing denominators, each \(\bar b_j\) is
461	  represented by some \(b_j \in B\) (writing \(1 \otimes b\) as \(b\)).
462	
463	  \emph{Step 2 — clear the integral-dependence denominators to a single \(g\).} Fix a
464	  finite set of algebra generators of \(B_K\) over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\); each
465	  generator satisfies an equation of integral dependence whose coefficients are
466	  polynomials in \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\), and there are finitely many such
467	  coefficient polynomials in total. Applying \cref{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator} to each
468	  coefficient polynomial yields a non-zero element of \(A\) over which that polynomial's
469	  coefficients become integral; folding over the finite collection (multiplying the
470	  finitely many elements) produces a single \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), such that
471	  \emph{every} coefficient of \emph{every} integral-dependence equation lies in
472	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). After inverting \(g\) all these integrality relations hold
473	  over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), and the chosen generators generate \(B_g\) as a module
474	  over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\); hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
475	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
476	
477	  \emph{Step 3 — AlgHom assembly.} The algebra homomorphism \(\varphi\) of the statement
478	  is the \(A_g\)-algebra map \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\) sending \(X_j \mapsto b_j\),
479	  where \(b_j \in B_g\) is the image of \(b_j \in B\). We establish injectivity and
480	  module-finiteness through a comparison with the situation over \(K\).
481	
482	  \emph{Step 3a — the comparison map \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\).} Since \(g \ne 0\) in the
483	  domain \(A\) and \(A \subseteq K\), the image of \(g\) is a unit of the field \(K\), so
484	  it is invertible in \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\). Hence the canonical \(A\)-algebra map
485	  \(B \to B_K\), \(b \mapsto 1 \otimes b\), sends \(g\) to a unit and, by the universal
486	  property of localisation, factors through a canonical \(A\)-algebra map
487	  \[
488	    \nu : B_g = B[g^{-1}] \longrightarrow B_K, \qquad \nu(b/g^k) = (1 \otimes b)\,(1 \otimes g)^{-k}.
489	  \]
490	  The map \(\nu\) is injective. Indeed, \(B\) is a domain, and both \(B_g\) and \(B_K\) are
491	  localisations of \(B\) at multiplicative subsets of the non-zerodivisors: \(B_g\) inverts
492	  the powers of \(g\), while \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\) inverts (in particular) the images of
493	  all non-zero elements of \(A\), a multiplicative set containing the powers of \(g\). A
494	  localisation of a domain at a set of non-zerodivisors is injective into any further such
495	  localisation. Concretely, the natural map \(B \to B_K\) is injective on the domain \(B\)
496	  (its kernel is the set of \(b\) annihilated by some non-zero element of \(A\), which in a

498	  \(1 \otimes b = 0\), hence \(b = 0\) and \(b/g^k = 0\). Under \(\nu\) the lifts

996	% for any three subsets $I$, $J$ and $K$ of $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of
997	% cardinality $d$, the co-cycle condition
998	% $\theta_{I,K}= \theta_{I,J}\theta_{J,K}$
999	% is satisfied."  (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,

1001	\begin{proof}
1002	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_transition_self, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_cocycle_theta_jk, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ik, lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq}
1003	  \leanok
1004	  We verify the scheme-morphism form
1005	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) by a direct matrix
1006	  computation, valid over the open locus of \(U^I\) on which all the minors
1007	  involved are invertible (namely \(P^I_J\) and \(P^I_K\), which suffices since
1008	  the maps are determined by their restriction to a dense open and all rings are
1009	  domains; the computation is an identity of matrices with entries in
1010	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J, 1/P^I_K]\)).
1011	
1012	  Start from the matrix \(X^I\) (the universal point of \(U^I\)). By
1013	  \cref{def:gr_transition},
1014	  \[
1015	    M^J \;:=\; \theta_{I,J}(X^I) \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
1016	  \]
1017	  a \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(J\)-minor is the identity, hence a point of
1018	  \(U^J\). Its \(K\)-minor is
1019	  \[
1020	    M^J_K \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_K,
1021	    \qquad\text{so}\qquad
1022	    (M^J_K)^{-1} \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J,
1023	  \]
1024	  using \((AB)^{-1} = B^{-1} A^{-1}\) for the invertible \(d \times d\) matrices
1025	  \(X^I_J\) and \(X^I_K\). Applying \(\theta_{J,K}\) to \(M^J\) gives
1026	  \[
1027	    \theta_{J,K}(M^J) \;=\; (M^J_K)^{-1} M^J
1028	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J \,\cdot\, (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I
1029	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I,
1030	  \]
1031	  the middle product \(X^I_J (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}\) cancelling by
1032	  associativity of matrix multiplication. The right-hand side is precisely
1033	  \(\theta_{I,K}(X^I) = (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I\). Thus
1034	  \(\theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,K}\) agree on the
1035	  universal matrix \(X^I\), hence agree as morphisms \(U^I \supseteq U^I_J \cap
1036	  U^I_K \to U^K\). The special case \(K = I\) (resp.\ \(J = I\)) together with
1037	  \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\) shows each \(\theta_{I,J}\) is an isomorphism onto its
1038	  image with inverse \(\theta_{J,I}\), since
1039	  \(\theta_{J,I} \circ \theta_{I,J} = \theta_{I,I} = \id\).
1040	\end{proof}

1042	\section{The glued Grassmannian scheme}
1043	\label{sec:gr_glued}
1044	
1045	\begin{definition}
1046	[The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
1047	  \label{def:gr_glued_scheme}
1048	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scheme}
1049	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
1050	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
1051	  % affine patches" (read from
1052	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L843-L850).
1053	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1054	  % "Therefore the schemes
1055	  %  $U^I$, as $I$ varies over all the
1056	  %  ${r\choose d}$ different
1057	  %  subsets of  $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of cardinality $d$,
1058	  %  can be glued together by the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$ to form a
1059	  %  finite-type scheme $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$. As each $U^I$ is
1060	  %  isomorphic to $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$, it follows that
1061	  %  $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is smooth of relative dimension $d(r-d)$."
1062	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
1063	  Gluing the affine charts \(\{U^I\}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}), one for each
1064	  of the \(\binom{r}{d}\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) of cardinality
1065	  \(d\), along the open subschemes \(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\) via the transition
1066	  isomorphisms \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I_J \xrightarrow{\sim} U^J_I\)
1067	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}) -- which satisfy the cocycle condition
1068	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\)
1069	  of \cref{lem:gr_cocycle} -- yields a scheme
1070	  \[
1071	    \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \longrightarrow \Spec \mathbb{Z}.
1072	  \]
1073	  It is of finite type over \(\mathbb{Z}\) (a finite gluing of finitely
1074	  generated \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebras), and under the gluing each \(U^I\) is an
1075	  open subscheme of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J\).
1076	
1077	  Moreover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is \emph{smooth of relative
1078	  dimension \(d(r-d)\)}. Smoothness and relative dimension are local on the
1079	  source; the charts \(\{U^I\}\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and each
1080	  \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) is
1081	  affine space of dimension \(d(r-d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\), which is smooth over
1082	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\) (the polynomial ring
1083	  \(\mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_{d(r-d)}]\) is a smooth \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra). As
1084	  the property may be checked on an open cover of the source, the conclusion
1085	  follows.
1086	\end{definition}

1089	\label{sec:gr_separated}

1091	\begin{lemma}
1092	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
1093	  \label{lem:gr_separated}
1094	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isSeparated}
1095	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition}
1096	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Separatedness" (read from
1097	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860).
1098	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1099	  % "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with $U^I\times U^J$
1100	  %  can be seen to be the closed subscheme $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$
1101	  %  defined by entries of the matrix formula $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$,
1102	  %  and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
1103	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
1104	  The structure morphism \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
1105	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is separated; equivalently, the diagonal
1106	  \(\Delta : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \times_{\mathbb{Z}}
1107	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is a closed immersion.
1108	\end{lemma}
1109	
1110	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with
1111	% $U^I\times U^J$ can be seen to be the closed subscheme
1112	% $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
1113	% $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$, and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
1114	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860.)
1115	\begin{proof}
1116	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition}
1117	  The products \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), as \(I, J\) range over the
1118	  size-\(d\) subsets, form an affine open cover of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)
1119	  \times_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Separatedness may be checked on this
1120	  cover: it suffices to show that for each pair \((I,J)\) the preimage
1121	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J)\) maps by a closed immersion into
1122	  \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), i.e.\ that the corresponding ring
1123	  homomorphism is surjective.
1124	
1125	  By \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme} the diagonal preimage is the overlap
1126	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J) = U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J = \Spec
1127	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\). The restricted diagonal corresponds to the ring map
1128	  \[
1129	    \delta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Z}[X^J]
1130	    \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J],
1131	    \qquad
1132	    X^I \otimes 1 \mapsto X^I, \quad 1 \otimes X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
1133	  \]
1134	  the second component being the comorphism \(\theta_{I,J}\) of
1135	  \cref{def:gr_transition} (a point of \(U^I_J\) and its image under
1136	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) are the two coordinates of a point of the diagonal). We claim
1137	  \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Its image contains all entries of \(X^I\)
1138	  (from the first factor). From the second factor it contains the entries of
1139	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\); taking the \(I\)-minor of this matrix, and using
1140	  \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\), the image contains the entries of \((X^I_J)^{-1}\),
1141	  and in particular
1142	  \[
1143	    \delta_{I,J}(1 \otimes P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = 1/P^I_J .
1144	  \]
1145	  Hence \(1/P^I_J\) lies in the image, so the image is all of
1146	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) and \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Concretely the
1147	  closed subscheme \(\Delta_{I,J} \subseteq U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\) is cut
1148	  out by the entries of the matrix relation
1149	  \[
1150	    X^J_I \, X^I - X^J \;=\; 0,
1151	  \]
1152	  which on the diagonal holds because \(X^J = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) forces
1153	  \(X^J_I = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) and therefore \(X^J_I X^I =
1154	  (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I = X^J\). As each restricted diagonal is a closed immersion,
1155	  \(\Delta\) is a closed immersion and \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over
1156	  \(\mathbb{Z}\).
1157	\end{proof}

1286	the absolute base \(\mathbb{Z}\) and establishes its smoothness, separatedness
1287	and properness. The following constructions are deferred to later chapters and

1294	    -- that it represents \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d) =
1295	    \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^r \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}} /
1296	    \mathbb{Z} / \mathbb{Z}}\); this is blueprinted as

1300	    free sheaf \(V\) of rank \(r\): the scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V,d)\) obtained by

507	  The localisation \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\) embeds in the fraction field, giving an
508	  injection of \(A_g\)-algebras \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\). Now suppose
509	  \(P \in A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is non-zero with \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\) in \(B_g\).
510	  Applying the injective map \(\nu\) and using \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\) together with the fact
511	  that \(\nu\) is an \(A\)-algebra map extending scalars to \(K\), we obtain
512	  \(P^K(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n) = 0\) in \(B_K\), where \(P^K\) is the image of \(P\)
513	  under the coefficient inclusion \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \hookrightarrow K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
514	  Since \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\) is injective, \(P \ne 0\) gives \(P^K \ne 0\); but the
515	  \(\bar b_j\) are algebraically independent over \(K\) (Step 1), so the only \(K\)-polynomial
516	  vanishing on them is \(0\), a contradiction. Hence no non-trivial relation exists, \((b_j)\)
517	  is algebraically independent over \(A_g\), and \(\varphi\) is injective.
518	
519	  \emph{Step 3c — module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\).} By Step 1,
520	  \(B_K\) is module-finite over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\); fix a finite set of algebra
521	  generators of \(B_K\) over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\) (Step 2). Each such generator
522	  satisfies a monic equation of integral dependence whose coefficients are polynomials in
523	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). The element \(g\) of Step 2 was chosen as a common
524	  denominator for the (finitely many) coefficient polynomials of these equations, so that
525	  after inverting \(g\) every such coefficient already lies in
526	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n] = \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Each chosen generator is the
527	  \(\nu\)-image of an element of \(B_g\), and its integral-dependence equation is the
528	  \(\nu\)-image of the corresponding monic equation holding in \(B_g\) with coefficients in
529	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\); since \(\nu\) is injective, that equation holds in \(B_g\)
530	  itself. Thus each generator of \(B_g\) is integral over the subalgebra
531	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\), and these generators generate \(B_g\) as an
532	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\)-algebra. A finite-type algebra that is integral over a
533	  subalgebra is module-finite over it; hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
534	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi = A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Transported along the injection
535	  \(\varphi\), this is exactly module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) for
536	  the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\), as claimed. This recovers the
537	  source's conclusion that \(B_g\) is finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
538	\end{proof}
539	
540	\begin{lemma}[injectivity of a localisation lift]
541	  \label{lem:gf_isLocalization_lift_injective}
542	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.isLocalization_lift_injective}
543	  Let \(S\) be a localisation of a ring \(R\) at a multiplicative set \(M\), and let
544	  \(g : R \to P\) be a ring homomorphism sending every element of \(M\) to a unit of
545	  \(P\), so that it induces the lift \(\widehat g : S \to P\). If both the localisation
546	  map \(R \to S\) and \(g\) itself are injective, then \(\widehat g\) is injective.
547	\end{lemma}
548	\begin{proof}
549	  The lift is characterised by \(\widehat g(r/m) = g(r)\,g(m)^{-1}\). By the universal
550	  criterion for injectivity of a localisation lift, \(\widehat g\) is injective iff for
551	  all \(r_1, r_2 \in R\) the equality of localisation images
552	  \((r_1 \mapsto S) = (r_2 \mapsto S)\) implies \(g(r_1) = g(r_2)\). Both the hypothesis
553	  and the conclusion of that implication reduce, via injectivity of \(R \to S\) and of
554	  \(g\), to the single condition \(r_1 = r_2\); hence the implication holds and
555	  \(\widehat g\) is injective. (This helper is applied twice in
556	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators}, to the comparison maps \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\)
557	  and \(\psi : A_g \to K\).)
558	\end{proof}
559	
560	\begin{lemma}
561	
562	[L5a — the generic-rank short exact sequence]
563	  \label{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses}
564	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_generic_rank_ses}
565	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
566	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1765).
567	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
568	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the
569	  %  domain $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact
570	  %  sequence of $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
571	  %  $$0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$$
572	  %  where $T$ is a finite torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
573	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
574	  %   Write P_d := MvPolynomial (Fin d) A.
575	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
576	  %       (d : ℕ) (N : Type*) [AddCommGroup N]
577	  %       [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
578	  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
579	  %       [Module A N] [IsScalarTower A (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N] :
580	  %       ∃ (m : ℕ) (φ : (Fin m → MvPolynomial (Fin d) A)
581	  %                        →ₗ[MvPolynomial (Fin d) A] N),
582	  %         Function.Injective φ ∧
583	  %         Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) (N ⧸ LinearMap.range φ)
584	  %   witness: m = Module.finrank (FractionRing P_d)
585	  %                  (LocalizedModule (nonZeroDivisors P_d) N), the generic rank;
586	  %            φ = Fintype.linearCombination P_d v, with v : Fin m → N the
587	  %            denominator-cleared lifts of a FractionRing P_d-basis of the localisation.
588	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (generic-rank SES).}
589	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(N\) a finite module over the
590	  polynomial ring \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure
591	  through the scalar tower \(A \to P_d \to N\)). Then there exist \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and
592	  an injective \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) whose cokernel
593	  \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\) is a torsion \(P_d\)-module. Equivalently, there is
594	  a short exact sequence of \(P_d\)-modules
595	  \[
596	    0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
597	    \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
598	  \]
599	
600	  The integer \(m\) is the \emph{generic rank} of \(N\): the dimension of the
601	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\)-vector space
602	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d) \otimes_{P_d} N\), i.e.\ of the localisation of \(N\) at the
603	  non-zero-divisors of the domain \(P_d\). This step lives over \(P_d\) directly —
604	  \emph{no inversion of any element \(g \in A\) is required here}; the element \(g\) of
605	  Nitsure's argument enters only at the reindexing step \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}.
606	\end{lemma}
607	
608	\begin{proof}
609	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1765).
610	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (generic-rank SES).}
611	  Write \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\); since \(A\) is a noetherian domain, so is \(P_d\)
612	  (Hilbert basis theorem, and a polynomial ring over a domain is a domain). Let
613	  \(Q = \mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\) be its fraction field and
614	  \(N_Q := Q \otimes_{P_d} N\) the localisation of \(N\) at the non-zero-divisors of
615	  \(P_d\); as \(N\) is finite over \(P_d\), \(N_Q\) is a finite-dimensional
616	  \(Q\)-vector space. Put \(m := \dim_Q N_Q\), the generic rank.
617	
618	  Choose a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\) and clear denominators: each basis vector is a
619	  \(Q\)-unit multiple of the image of some \(v_i \in N\), so the images of
620	  \(v_1, \dots, v_m \in N\) again form a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\). In particular the family
621	  \((v_i)_{i}\) is linearly independent over \(Q\); restricting scalars along the
622	  injection \(P_d \hookrightarrow Q\), it is linearly independent over \(P_d\). Let
623	  \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) be the \(P_d\)-linear map
624	  \((c_1, \dots, c_m) \mapsto \sum_i c_i v_i\). Linear independence of \((v_i)\) over
625	  \(P_d\) makes \(\varphi\) injective.
626	
627	  Set \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Because the images of \(v_1, \dots, v_m\)
628	  already span \(N_Q\) over \(Q\), the localised map \(\varphi \otimes Q : Q^{\oplus m}
629	  \to N_Q\) is an isomorphism, so \(Q \otimes_{P_d} T = 0\). A finite \(P_d\)-module whose
630	  localisation at the non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\) vanishes is torsion; hence \(T\) is a
631	  torsion \(P_d\)-module. This is the asserted short exact sequence.
632	\end{proof}

644	\begin{lemma}[L5b anchor — a finite torsion module has a non-zero-divisor annihilator]
645	  \label{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}
646	  \lean{Submodule.annihilator_top_inter_nonZeroDivisors}
647	  \mathlibok
648	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case $n=-1$
649	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1724-L1727)
650	  % -- the same torsion-annihilation primitive, here provided in general by Mathlib.
651	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
652	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
653	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating
654	  %  elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$."
655	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
656	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and \(M\) a finite torsion \(R\)-module. Then the
657	  annihilator of \(M\) meets the non-zero-divisors of \(R\): the set
658	  \((\top : \operatorname{Submodule} R\,M).\mathrm{annihilator} \cap R^{\circ}\) is
659	  non-empty, where \(R^{\circ}\) denotes the non-zero-divisors of \(R\).
660	\end{lemma}
661	
662	\begin{lemma}[L5b.1 — annihilator extraction for the torsion module]
663	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator}
664	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_torsion_annihilator}
665	  \uses{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}
666	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case $n=-1$
667	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1724-L1727).
668	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
669	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
670	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating
671	  %  elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$."
672	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
673	  %   Write P_d := MvPolynomial (Fin d) A. The conclusion is the existence of a
674	  %   non-zero annihilator; the downstream consequence "T is finite over P_d/(F)" is
675	  %   left to the assembly (a surjection P_d ↠ P_d/(F) pushes a finite generating set).
676	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (d : ℕ)
677	  %       (T : Type*) [AddCommGroup T] [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
678	  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
679	  %       (htors : Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T) :
680	  %       ∃ F : MvPolynomial (Fin d) A, F ≠ 0 ∧
681	  %         F ∈ Module.annihilator (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T
682	  %   (F ∈ annihilator unfolds to ∀ x : T, F • x = 0.)
683	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (torsion annihilation).}
684	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
685	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\). Then there exists a non-zero
686	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\); consequently the \(P_d\)-action on \(T\) factors
687	  through \(P_d/(F)\) and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
688	\end{lemma}
689	
690	\begin{proof}
691	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1724-L1727).
692	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (torsion annihilation).}
693	  The ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is a noetherian domain (a polynomial ring over the
694	  noetherian domain \(A\)), and \(T\) is a finite torsion \(P_d\)-module. By
695	  \cref{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors} the annihilator of \(T\) meets the
696	  non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\); choose \(F\) in that intersection. In the domain \(P_d\) a
697	  non-zero-divisor is exactly a non-zero element, so \(F \ne 0\), and \(F \cdot T = 0\).
698	  Since \(F\) annihilates \(T\), the \(P_d\)-module structure descends along the quotient
699	  map \(P_d \twoheadrightarrow P_d/(F)\); the image of any finite \(P_d\)-generating set of
700	  \(T\) generates \(T\) over \(P_d/(F)\), so \(T\) is finite over \(P_d/(F)\).
701	\end{proof}

703	\subsubsection{Nagata change-of-variables machinery}
704	\label{sec:gf_nagata_machinery}
705	
706	The Nagata normalisation lemma \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} is assembled from
707	technical helper lemmas implementing the triangular change of variables and the
708	radix-encoding degree bookkeeping, following the Nagata trick of
709	\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 over a noetherian domain. Throughout, \(k\) is a domain,
710	\(n \ge 0\), and \(f \in k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is a fixed polynomial; we write
711	\(\mathrm{up} := 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}}(f)\) and the radix weights \(r_i := \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\)
712	for \(i = 0, \dots, n\) (so every exponent occurring in \(f\) is a base-\(\mathrm{up}\) digit).
713	The distinguished variable is \(X_0\).
714	
715	\begin{definition}[Nagata substitution \(T_1\)]
716	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T1}
717	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T1}
718	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
719	  For a scalar \(c \in k\), the substitution \(T_1(c)\) is the \(k\)-algebra endomorphism
720	  of \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) determined on generators by
721	  \[
722	    X_0 \mapsto X_0, \qquad X_i \mapsto X_i + c\,X_0^{\,r_i} \quad (i \ne 0).
723	  \]
724	  It is triangular in the variables \(X_i\) (\(i \ne 0\)) over the distinguished variable
725	  \(X_0\); this is the device realising the support-dimension drop.
726	\end{definition}
727	
728	\begin{lemma}[\(T_1\) is invertible by the opposite shift]
729	  \label{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
730	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.t1_comp_t1_neg}
731	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
732	  For every \(c \in k\), \(T_1(c) \circ T_1(-c) = \mathrm{id}\); the Nagata substitution is
733	  invertible, with inverse the substitution of opposite coefficient.
734	\end{lemma}
735	\begin{proof}
736	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
737	  It suffices to check the two composites agree on each generator: \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), and
738	  \(X_i \mapsto X_i + (-c)X_0^{r_i} + c\,X_0^{r_i} = X_i\) for \(i \ne 0\). Proved directly
739	  in Lean.
740	\end{proof}
741	
742	\begin{definition}[Nagata automorphism \(T\)]
743	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T}
744	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T}
745	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1, lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
746	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
747	  The Nagata change of variables is the \(k\)-algebra automorphism \(T := T_1(1)\) of
748	  \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) — i.e.\ \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{\,r_i}\) (\(i \ne 0\)) and
749	  \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\) — packaged as an algebra equivalence with inverse \(T_1(-1)\) via
750	  \cref{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}.
751	\end{definition}
752	
753	\begin{lemma}[Exponent bound is preserved by listing]
754	  \label{lem:gf_lt_up}
755	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.lt_up}
756	  If every entry of an exponent vector \(v\) satisfies \(v_i < \mathrm{up}\), then
757	  every entry of the list of values of \(v\) is also \(< \mathrm{up}\).
758	\end{lemma}
759	\begin{proof}
760	  Proved directly in Lean.
761	\end{proof}
762	
763	\begin{lemma}[Injectivity of the radix encoding]
764	  \label{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
765	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.sum_r_mul_ne}
766	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
767	  Let \(v, w\) be exponent vectors with all entries \(< \mathrm{up}\) and \(v \ne w\). Then
768	  the weighted base-\(\mathrm{up}\) sums differ:
769	  \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\), where \(r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\).
770	\end{lemma}
771	\begin{proof}
772	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
773	  The map \(v \mapsto \sum_i \mathrm{up}^{\,i} v_i\) is the value of the base-\(\mathrm{up}\)
774	  digit string \(v\); since every digit is \(< \mathrm{up}\) (\cref{lem:gf_lt_up}), this
775	  encoding is injective by uniqueness of base-\(\mathrm{up}\) expansions, so distinct
776	  \(v \ne w\) give distinct sums.
777	\end{proof}
778	
779	\begin{lemma}[\(X_0\)-degree of a transformed monomial]
780	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t}
781	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_zero_t}
782	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
783	  For a non-zero scalar \(a \ne 0\) and exponent vector \(v\), the degree in \(X_0\) of the
784	  transformed monomial is the radix value
785	  \[
786	    \deg_{X_0}\!\big(T(a\,X^v)\big) = \sum_i r_i v_i, \qquad r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}.
787	  \]
788	\end{lemma}
789	\begin{proof}
790	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
791	  Under \(T\), \(a\,X^v \mapsto a\,X_0^{v_0}\prod_{i \ne 0}(X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\). Read as
792	  a polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), each factor
793	  \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) has \(X_0\)-degree \(r_i v_i\) and leading coefficient \(1\);
794	  the degrees add to \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) (the hypothesis \(a \ne 0\) keeps the top term from
795	  vanishing over the domain \(k\)).
796	\end{proof}
797	
798	\begin{lemma}[Distinct monomials acquire distinct \(X_0\)-degrees]
799	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
800	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
801	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
802	  For distinct support exponents \(v \ne w\) of \(f\), the transformed monomials
803	  \(T(c_v X^v)\) and \(T(c_w X^w)\) (where \(c_v, c_w\) are the corresponding coefficients
804	  of \(f\)) have distinct degrees in \(X_0\), making the top \(X_0\)-degree of \(T(f)\) unique.
805	\end{lemma}
806	\begin{proof}
807	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
808	  By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t} the two degrees are \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) and
809	  \(\sum_i r_i w_i\). Each support exponent is bounded by the total degree of \(f\), hence
810	  \(< \mathrm{up} = 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}} f\); radix-injectivity
811	  \cref{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne} then yields \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\).
812	\end{proof}
813	
814	\begin{lemma}[Leading coefficient of a transformed monomial]
815	  \label{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
816	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
817	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
818	  For any exponent \(v\) with coefficient \(c_v := [X^v]\,f\), viewing \(T(c_v X^v)\) as a
819	  univariate polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), its leading coefficient in
820	  \(X_0\) is the constant \(c_v \in k\).
821	\end{lemma}
822	\begin{proof}
823	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
824	  Each factor \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) is monic in \(X_0\) with leading coefficient
825	  \(1\); a product of monics is monic, so the leading coefficient of \(T(c_v X^v)\) in
826	  \(X_0\) is exactly the scalar \(c_v\).
827	\end{proof}
828	
829	\begin{lemma}[Leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) is a non-zero constant]
830	  \label{lem:gf_T_leadingcoeff_eq}
831	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T_leadingcoeff_eq}
832	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
833	  Suppose \(f \ne 0\). Then there is a support exponent \(v \in \mathrm{supp}\,f\) such that
834	  the leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) in \(X_0\) equals the constant \(c_v := [X^v]\,f \ne 0\).
835	  Thus \(T(f)\) is, up to the non-zero scalar \(c_v\), monic in \(X_0\).

1047	        lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic, lem:gf_pullback_module_transport,
1048	        lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv, lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
1049	  Write \(d = (d-1) + 1\) and \(P_d = A[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) (the distinguished variable
1050	  \(X_0\) is the one eliminated in the single-variable step; cf.\ \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}).

1052	  \emph{Annihilator.} By \cref{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator} there is a non-zero
1053	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\), and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
1054	
1055	  \emph{Nagata normalisation.} Applying \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} to \(F \ne 0\)
1056	  yields a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(P_d\) and a non-zero
1057	  \(g \in A\) such that the image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) has a unit
1058	  leading coefficient in \(X_0\). The automorphism \(e\) induces a ring isomorphism
1059	  \(P_d/(F) \cong P_d/(e\,F)\), so \(T\) is also finite over \(P_d/(e\,F)\). Localising at
1060	  the powers of \(g\) (localisation commutes with the quotient), \(T_g\) is finite over
1061	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(e\,F) = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\).

1064	  \cref{lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic} with \(R = A_g\) and \(p\) the image of
1065	  \(e(F)\) (whose leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is a unit): the quotient
1066	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\) is module-finite over the coefficient subring
1067	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), a polynomial ring in \(m' := d - 1\) variables over
1068	  \(A_g\).

1070	  \emph{Localisation transport.} The elimination step records module-finiteness of the
1071	  \emph{quotient ring} over its coefficient subring, but the conclusion of the lemma is
1072	  about a localisation of the torsion module \(T\). Making the two agree is the
1073	  mathematical content of this step, which proceeds as follows.
1074	
1075	  Two distinct localisations of \(T\) are in play and must be identified. The natural
1076	  setting for the elimination is the localisation \(T'_g := T[\,\mathcal{C}^{-1}]\) of \(T\)
1077	  at the multiplicative set \(\mathcal{C} = \{\,c \cdot g^{\,k} : k \ge 0\,\}\) of constant
1078	  polynomials \(g^k\) inside \(P_d\); this localisation carries, for free, the full
1079	  module structure over the localised polynomial ring
1080	  \(P_d[g^{-1}] = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), its finiteness, and the quotient structure by
1081	  \((e\,F)\). The \emph{goal}, however, is phrased with \(T_g := T[g^{-1}]\), the
1082	  localisation of \(T\) at the powers of the single element \(g \in A\). One first proves
1083	  \(T'_g\) module-finite over \(R := A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) and then transports that
1084	  finiteness to \(T_g\).
1085	
1086	  The change of variables \(e\) is transported to the localised polynomial ring as a
1087	  \emph{ring isomorphism} \(\bar e : P_d[g^{-1}] \xrightarrow{\sim} P_d[g^{-1}]\), obtained
1088	  by localising \(e\) at \(\mathcal{C}\) (which \(e\) preserves, since \(e\) fixes the
1089	  constants \(c\,X^0\)); it carries the localised annihilator \(F_g\) to the
1090	  Nagata-normalised generator \(G\) and fixes the constants coming from the base. Passing
1091	  to quotients, \(\bar e\) descends to a ring isomorphism
1092	  \(\psi : P_d[g^{-1}]/(F_g) \xrightarrow{\sim} P_d[g^{-1}]/(G)\) of the two quotient rings
1093	  (using that the image of the ideal \((F_g)\) under \(\bar e\) is \((G)\)). Both quotient
1094	  rings act on \(T'_g\), and module-finiteness over \(R\) of the elimination-side quotient
1095	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(G)\) is transported across \(\psi\) — a ring isomorphism of the
1096	  \emph{acting} ring compatible with the fixed \(R\)-algebra structure — by
1097	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv}, yielding \(T'_g\) module-finite over \(R\).
1098	
1099	  Finally the two localisations are identified. Restricting scalars of the canonical
1100	  \(P_d\)-localisation map of \(T\) along \(A \to P_d\) exhibits \(T'_g\) as a localisation
1101	  of \(T\) \emph{at the powers of \(g\) over \(A\)} — this descent of the
1102	  localised-module property along the scalar tower is
1103	  \cref{lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars} — so the universal property produces a
1104	  linear isomorphism \(T_g \cong T'_g\) identifying the two.
1105	
1106	  \emph{Action-diamond subtlety.} The conclusion of the lemma requires an
1107	  \(A_g\)-module structure on \(T_g\) sitting in a scalar tower
1108	  \(A_g \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}] \to T_g\). One cannot simply install an anonymous
1109	  \(A_g\)-action by fiat: \(T_g = T[g^{-1}]\) already carries its \emph{canonical}
1110	  localisation action of \(A_g\), and this canonical action is the one the scalar-tower
1111	  requirement sees. Hence the identification \(T_g \cong T'_g\) must be promoted to an
1112	  \(A_g\)-linear isomorphism — which it is, because the transported action agrees with the
1113	  canonical localisation action on the \(A_g\)-constants: the constant
1114	  \(\mathcal{C}\)-action of \(A_g\) on \(T'_g\) factors through the original \(A\)-action on
1115	  \(T\) exactly as the canonical \(g\)-localisation action does. With the isomorphism made
1116	  \(A_g\)-linear, the \(R\)-module structure and finiteness pull back from \(T'_g\) to
1117	  \(T_g\) by \cref{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}, and the scalar tower
1118	  \(A_g \to R \to T_g\) follows.

1122	  required reindexing; no Krull-dimension theory is needed, the number of variables
1123	  dropping by exactly one through the single-variable elimination.
1124	\end{proof}

1126	\subsubsection{Module-structure transport helpers (reindex)}
1127	\label{sec:gf_reindex_transport_helpers}
1128	
1129	The localisation-transport step of \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} moves a module structure
1130	and its finiteness across (i) an additive equivalence of the underlying groups, (ii) a ring
1131	isomorphism of the acting ring, and (iii) a change of localising monoid along a scalar
1132	tower. The following helper lemmas package those three transport principles. They are general
1133	module-theory facts; no external source is cited.
1134	
1135	\begin{lemma}[Module transport along an additive equivalence]
1136	  \label{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}
1137	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
1138	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
1139	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullback_isScalarTower}
1140	  Let \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} N\) be an additive equivalence and let \(M\) carry an
1141	  \(R\)-module structure. Pulling the action back along \(e\) by
1142	  \(r \cdot y := e\big(r \cdot e^{-1}(y)\big)\) makes \(N\) an \(R\)-module for which \(e\)
1143	  is \(R\)-linear. Under this transported structure: (a) if \(M\) is module-finite over
1144	  \(R\) then so is \(N\); and (b) if \(M\) additionally carries a compatible action of a
1145	  base ring \(A_g\) with scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to M\), then the pulled-back actions on
1146	  \(N\) again form a scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to N\).
1147	\end{lemma}
1148	\begin{proof}
1149	  The module axioms for \(y \mapsto e(r \cdot e^{-1}(y))\) follow termwise from those on
1150	  \(M\) together with the fact that \(e\) and \(e^{-1}\) are additive and mutually inverse;
1151	  by construction \(e\) is then an \(R\)-linear isomorphism. Statement (a) follows by
1152	  transporting module-finiteness across the \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(e\).
1153	  Statement (b) checks the scalar-tower identity
1154	  \((a\,r)\cdot y = a\cdot(r\cdot y)\) on \(N\) by transporting it across \(e\) and applying
1155	  associativity of the scalar action on \(M\).
1156	\end{proof}
1157	
1158	\begin{lemma}[Finite transport along a ring isomorphism of the acting ring]
1159	  \label{lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv}
1160	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_quotientRingEquiv}
1161	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(\psi : B_1 \xrightarrow{\sim} B_2\) be a ring
1162	  isomorphism between two \(R\)-algebras that is compatible with the structure maps, i.e.\
1163	  \(\psi \circ (\text{algebra map } R \to B_1) = (\text{algebra map } R \to B_2)\). If
1164	  \(B_2\) is module-finite over \(R\) and \(M\) is module-finite over \(B_1\) (with the
1165	  scalar tower \(R \to B_1 \to M\)), then \(M\) is module-finite over \(R\).
1166	\end{lemma}
1167	\begin{proof}
1168	  Compatibility of \(\psi^{-1}\) with the structure maps follows from that of \(\psi\), so
1169	  \(\psi^{-1}\) is an \(R\)-algebra isomorphism; transporting \(R\)-finiteness of \(B_2\)
1170	  across it makes \(B_1\) module-finite over \(R\). Transitivity of module-finiteness through
1171	  the scalar tower \(R \to B_1 \to M\) then gives \(M\) module-finite over \(R\).
1172	\end{proof}
1173	
1174	\begin{lemma}[Descent of a localised-module structure along a scalar tower]
1175	  \label{lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
1176	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.isLocalizedModule_restrictScalars}
1177	  Let \(A_g \to R\) be a ring map with compatible \(A_g\)- and \(R\)-module structures on
1178	  \(M\) and \(M'\) (scalar towers \(A_g \to R \to M\) and \(A_g \to R \to M'\)), and let
1179	  \(S \subseteq A_g\) be a multiplicative subset. If an \(R\)-linear map
1180	  \(f : M \to M'\) realises \(M'\) as the localisation of \(M\) at the image submonoid
1181	  \(S \cdot 1 \subseteq R\) over \(R\), then its restriction of scalars to \(A_g\) realises
1182	  \(M'\) as the localisation of \(M\) at \(S\) over \(A_g\).
1183	\end{lemma}
1184	\begin{proof}
1185	  One verifies the universal property of a localised module for the restricted map. Each
1186	  \(s \in S\) acts invertibly on \(M'\): its action coincides, via the scalar tower, with
1187	  that of its image in \(R\), which is a unit because \(f\) is a localisation at the image
1188	  submonoid. Surjectivity onto \(M'\) by fractions \(x/s\) with \(s \in S\) and the
1189	  equality criterion likewise descend from the corresponding image-submonoid statements,
1190	  rewriting each \(R\)-scalar by an \(A_g\)-scalar through the scalar tower. Assembling
1191	  these conditions gives the claim.
1192	\end{proof}
1193	
1194	\begin{lemma}[Localisation identification across an associated denominator]
1195	  \label{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated}
1196	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.mul_of_associated}
1197	  \mathlibok
1198	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1199	  Let \(R \to S \to T\) be a tower of commutative rings with compatible structure maps, let
1200	  \(x, z \in R\) and \(y \in S\), and suppose \(S\) is the localisation of \(R\) away from
1201	  \(x\) and \(T\) is the localisation of \(S\) away from \(y\). If the image of \(z\) in \(S\)
1202	  is associated to \(y\) (the two differ by a unit), then \(T\) is the localisation of \(R\)
1203	  away from the single product \(x z\); that is, \(T \cong R[(x z)^{-1}]\).
1204	\end{lemma}
1205	
1206	\begin{lemma}[Freeness transports across a semilinear ring isomorphism]
1207	  \label{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1208	  \lean{Module.Free.of_ringEquiv}
1209	  \mathlibok
1210	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1211	  Let \(R, R'\) be rings, \(M\) an \(R\)-module and \(M'\) an \(R'\)-module, and let
1212	  \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M'\) be an additive isomorphism that is semilinear over a ring
1213	  isomorphism \(\sigma : R \xrightarrow{\sim} R'\), i.e.\ \(e(r \cdot m) = \sigma(r) \cdot e(m)\).
1214	  If \(M\) is free over \(R\), then \(M'\) is free over \(R'\).
1215	\end{lemma}

1217	\begin{lemma}[L5 step-4 helper — descent of freeness across a tower of \(\mathrm{Away}\) localisations]
1218	  \label{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1219	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_away_tower}
1220	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1221	  % LEAN DEPS: the localisation-transport primitives the Lean proof consumes are
1222	  %   IsLocalization.surj                          (clear the denominator of h over A_g)
1223	  %   IsLocalization.map_units                     (the cleared denominator s ∈ powers g is a unit)
1224	  %   IsLocalization.Away.mul_of_associated        (key ring identification: Away(g·a) on A_h)
1225	  %   IsLocalization.algEquiv                      (ring iso Away(g·a) ≅ A_h)
1226	  %   Module.Free.of_ringEquiv                     (transport freeness across that ring iso)
1227	  %   IsLocalizedModule.linearEquiv                (the σ-semilinear module equivalence)
1228	  %   LinearEquiv.extendScalarsOfIsLocalization    (upgrade the R-linear equiv to Away(g·a)-linear)
1229	  % The one primitive NOT packaged by Mathlib — that the composite localisation map
1230	  %   ψ := mkLinearMap(powers h) ∘ mkLinearMap(powers g) : T → (T_g)_h
1231	  % satisfies IsLocalizedModule (powers (g·a)) ψ — is proved directly from the three
1232	  % IsLocalizedModule axioms (units act invertibly, surjectivity up to denominators, the
1233	  % equality criterion); there is no packaged localisation-of-localisation lemma to invoke.
1234	  % NOTE: the closed Lean proof DID find a packaged route — the composite-localisation
1235	  % `IsLocalizedModule (powers (g·a)) ψ` step goes through `IsBaseChange.comp` via
1236	  % `isLocalizedModule_iff_isBaseChange`, so the "no packaged lemma" remark above is superseded.
1237	  % WITNESS: the conclusion holds at the SINGLE product f := g·a (not at a square f²).
1238	  Let \(A\) be a domain and \(T\) an \(A\)-module. Let \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), and let
1239	  \(h \in A_g := \mathrm{Away}(g)\) be non-zero. Write
1240	  \((T_g)_h\) for the iterated localisation of \(T\) — first away from \(g\), then away from
1241	  \(h\) — and \((A_g)_h := \mathrm{Away}(h)\) for the corresponding iterated localisation of the
1242	  base ring. If \((T_g)_h\) is free over \((A_g)_h\), then there exists \(f \in A\),
1243	  \(f \ne 0\), such that the single localisation \(T_f\) is free over \(A_f := \mathrm{Away}(f)\).
1244	  Concretely the witness is the single product \(f := g\,a\) for an element \(a \in A\) whose
1245	  image in \(A_g\) is associated to \(h\).
1246	\end{lemma}
1247	
1248	\begin{proof}
1249	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1250	  \emph{Clear the denominator of \(h\).} As \(h\) lies in \(A_g = \mathrm{Away}(g)\),
1251	  surjectivity of the localisation map \(A \to A_g\) onto fractions yields \(a \in A\) and a
1252	  power \(s\) of \(g\) with \(h \cdot \bar s = \bar a\) in \(A_g\), where \(\bar{\,\cdot\,}\)
1253	  denotes the image in \(A_g\). The image \(\bar s\) is a unit (a power of \(g\) is invertible
1254	  in \(A_g\)), so \(\bar a = h\,\bar s\) is associated to \(h\); in particular \(\bar a \ne 0\)
1255	  (because \(h \ne 0\)), hence \(a \ne 0\). Since \(A\) is a domain and \(g, a \ne 0\), the
1256	  product \(f := g\,a\) is non-zero.
1257	
1258	  \emph{Identify the base rings.} The associatedness of \(\bar a\) and \(h\), together with the
1259	  tower \(A \to A_g \to A_h\), identifies the iterated localisation
1260	  \((A_g)_h = \mathrm{Away}(h)\) with the single localisation of \(A\) away from the product
1261	  \(g\,a\): by \cref{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated} (with \(x = g\), \(z = a\),
1262	  \(y = h\)) the ring \(A_h\) is a localisation of \(A\) away from \(g\,a\), and uniqueness of
1263	  localisation furnishes a ring isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \xrightarrow{\sim} A_h\).
1264	
1265	  \emph{Identify the modules.} The composite localisation map
1266	  \[
1267	    \psi : T \longrightarrow (T_g)_h, \qquad
1268	    \psi := \mathrm{mk}_{\langle h\rangle} \circ \mathrm{mk}_{\langle g\rangle},
1269	  \]
1270	  first into \(T_g\) and then into \((T_g)_h\), realises \((T_g)_h\) as the localisation of
1271	  \(T\) at the powers of \(g\,a\): every \(g^n a^m\) acts invertibly on \((T_g)_h\), \(\psi\) is
1272	  surjective up to such denominators, and its kernel is the expected one. This
1273	  localisation-of-localisation identity is verified directly from the defining properties of a
1274	  localised module; no packaged lemma supplies it.
1275	
1276	  \emph{Transport freeness.} By hypothesis \((T_g)_h\) is free over \(A_h\). The ring
1277	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \cong A_h\) and the induced semilinear module equivalence
1278	  \(T_{g\,a} \simeq (T_g)_h\) — assembled from the localised-module identification of the
1279	  previous step and promoted to an \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\)-linear equivalence — carry this
1280	  freeness back by \cref{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}: \(T_{g\,a}\) is free over
1281	  \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\). Taking \(f := g\,a\) gives \(T_f\) free over \(A_f\), as required.
1282	\end{proof}

1318	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,

1323	  % TOGETHER WITH N. Formally, prove
1324	  %   ∀ d, ∀ (A) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (N) [..module data..],
1325	  %        ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧ Module.Free (Localization.Away f)
1326	  %                              (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) N)

1329	  % This is load-bearing, and is the real cause of the prior stall: the reindex

1331	  % the inductive hypothesis must be applied at base A_g, NOT A. An IH that fixes A would
1332	  % not typecheck on the reindexed module T_g (which is finite over
1333	  % MvPolynomial (Fin m') A_g). A_g remains a noetherian domain (a localisation of a
1334	  % noetherian domain), so the motive's hypotheses hold at A_g. Because `A` is already an
1335	  % explicit argument of the pinned signature, this is a reversion of A into the motive,
1336	  % NOT a change to the public signature. See analogies/gf-generic-rank-ses.md.
1337	  We argue by strong induction on \(d\), \emph{generalising the base domain \(A\)} (along
1338	  with the module \(N\)): the inductive hypothesis is available for every \(d' < d\),
1339	  every noetherian domain in place of \(A\), and every finite module over the
1340	  corresponding polynomial ring.

1343	  \(N\) is a finite \(A\)-module and the claim is exactly the finite-module leaf
1344	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}.

1347	  \(A\) — equivalently \(N_K = 0\) for \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) — then the torsion base
1348	  case \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} produces \(f \ne 0\) with \(N_f = 0\), which is free
1349	  over \(A_f\). Otherwise we proceed in five steps.

1353	      \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) with torsion cokernel
1354	      \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\), i.e.\ a short exact sequence of
1355	      \(P_d\)-modules
1356	      \[
1357	        0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
1358	        \qquad T \text{ torsion}.

1360	      No element of \(A\) is inverted at this step.
1361	    \item \textbf{Reindex the torsion cokernel.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} to the
1362	      finite torsion module \(T\): there are \(g \ne 0\) in \(A\) and \(m' < d\) such that
1363	      the localisation \(T_g\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
1364	      \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in fewer variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\).
1365	    \item \textbf{Inductive hypothesis at the new base \(A_g\).} Since \(A_g\) is again a
1366	      noetherian domain and \(m' < d\), the inductive hypothesis applies \emph{at base

1369	      point at which the base ring of the induction must be allowed to change from \(A\)

1377	      \(f := g a \ne 0\) — the single product, \emph{not} a square — so that \(T_f\) is free
1378	      over \(A_f\).

1380	      % (free_localizationAway_of_away_tower) is now CLOSED and axiom-clean. The L5 sorry that

1388	      % without that instance-alignment refactor first.
1389	    \item \textbf{Splice.} The left end \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) of the sequence is a free
1390	      \(A\)-module (the polynomial ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is \(A\)-free, hence
1391	      so is any finite direct power; note \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) is \emph{not} module-finite
1392	      over \(A\), but freeness is all the splice requires), and a free module stays free
1393	      under the localisation \(A \to A_f\); so \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) localises to a free
1394	      \(A_f\)-module. With both ends free over \(A_f\) after inverting \(f = g a\),
1395	      \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact} splices the localised short exact sequence to

1397	      completes the induction.

1442	\begin{theorem}
1443	
1444

1481	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
1482	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
1483	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
1484	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
1485	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.

1488	  need not become flat over any non-empty open of \(S\).

1492	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
1493	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
1494	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from

1498	\begin{proof}
1499	  
1500	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\), where \(A\) is a
1501	  noetherian domain (\(S\) integral and locally noetherian). We assemble the witness
1502	  \(V\) from the algebraic form on a finite affine cover.
1503	
1504	  \emph{Step 1 — finite affine cover above \(U_0\).} Since \(p\) is locally of finite
1505	  type it is in particular quasi-compact, so the open subscheme \(X_{U_0} = p^{-1}(U_0)\)
1506	  is quasi-compact; choose a finite affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_{j}\) of
1507	  \(X_{U_0}\) by affine opens of \(X\). Because \(p\) is locally of finite type, each
1508	  ring \(B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\) is a finite-type \(A\)-algebra.
1509	
1510	  \emph{Step 2 — read off a finite module on each patch.} On each \(W_j\), quasi-coherence
1511	  of \(\F\) identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of the
1512	  section module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and the finite-type hypothesis makes \(M_j\)
1513	  a finite \(B_j\)-module. Through the scalar tower \(A \to B_j \to M_j\), \(M_j\) is
1514	  the data fed to the algebraic form.
1515	
1516	  \emph{Step 3 — apply the algebraic form and take the common basic open.}
1517	  \cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} applied to \((A, B_j, M_j)\) yields \(f_j \in A\),
1518	  \(f_j \ne 0\), with \((M_j)_{f_j}\) free over \(A_{f_j}\). As the cover is finite, set
1519	  \(f := \prod_j f_j \ne 0\) (a non-zero element of the domain \(A\)) and
1520	  \(V := D(f) \subseteq \Spec A \subseteq S\), a non-empty basic open; over \(V\) every
1521	  \((M_j)_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1522	
1523	  \emph{Step 4 — freeness implies flatness.} On each patch the localized section module
1524	  is free, hence flat over \(A_f\); the fibrewise flatness conclusion then follows from
1525	  these local freeness statements by the criterion that a module flat at every maximal
1526	  ideal is flat. This produces the required \(V\) with \(\F|_{X_V}\) flat over \(\OO_V\).
1527	\end{proof}

19	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
20	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
21	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
22	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
23	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
24	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
25	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
26	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
27	single non-zero element of the base ring.

29	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
30	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
31	\(\F\).

39	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
40	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type

### 1. Picard_QuotScheme.tex — GATE-CRITICAL

**`complete: true` | `correct: true`**

#### 1a. `lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero` — newly written this iter

**Block completeness and correctness:** The proof sketches Route (b): the argument lives entirely inside Q₀'s κ-structure. For each degree n, the image of ψ\_n lands in the degree-n graded piece of Q₀ (a finite-dimensional κ-vector space). The family `(range(ψ_n))_{n : ℕ}` is iSupIndep: graded pieces of the ambient M are independent by the ambient `DirectSum.Decomposition`, and ψ\_n maps M\_n into Q₀\_n by construction, so independence of the ambient graded pieces pulls back to independence of the images. `finite_ne_bot_of_iSupIndep` (`lem:finite_ne_bot_of_iSupIndep_mathlib`) then yields finitely many n with non-zero image, giving eventual vanishing of the Hilbert function difference.

This is concrete and formalizable: it stays inside the ambient pair-of-submodules framework (no derived carrier/graded quotient ring), uses only κ-linear algebra on finite-dimensional pieces, and avoids the Route (a) scalar-ring incompatibility (Q₀ → M/N' would require a ring map κ → base ring that doesn't canonically exist).
